rationales.security: She has an 'emergency fund fully topped up' and a separate apartment account, clearly organizing her life around protection and long-term stability.
rationales.benevolence: The life she imagines is still geographically and emotionally tied to home, with the apartment image centered on staying close to family rather than pulling away from them.

rationales.self_direction: She says it feels like she is 'filling in a template someone else drew,' which directly signals movement along an externally defined path.
rationales.stimulation: Spending three hours color-coding binders and choosing nearby apartments for practical reasons reflects comfort with order and routine over novelty.
rationales.security: The easy commute and practical neighborhood choice are exactly the kind of stable, low-risk reasons she finds persuasive.
rationales.conformity: She expects she will go to the open houses because 'my dad will ask about it and I'll feel guilty if I haven't,' showing compliance with family expectation.
rationales.tradition: She keeps 'Sunday dinners' and living near her parents at the center of the housing decision, preserving familiar family routines.

rationales.self_direction: He discards the requested session model and builds the token flow that 'would actually work,' privileging his own technical judgment over instructions.
rationales.achievement: He finished the module 'ahead of schedule' and has 'benchmark numbers ready,' showing a strong focus on competent performance.
rationales.conformity: He knowingly ignores the client's specified approach after getting no response, rather than simply doing what was asked.

rationales.self_direction: He emphasizes that he 'pick[s] my own projects' and took three weeks off without permission, framing autonomy as central to how he measures a good life.
rationales.conformity: He resists his family's idea of a 'real company' career and refuses to let their office-and-title yardstick define his path.

rationales.self_direction: The on-site requirement bothers him because of the 'loss of control over my own hours,' making autonomy the clear core concern.
rationales.conformity: He rejects the standard '9 to 5' structure as 'a stupid way to work,' pushing back against conventional workplace expectations.

rationales.self_direction: He is already preparing an 'exit plan' because the imposed office structure conflicts with 'what makes you actually function well,' showing he protects autonomy even under pressure.
rationales.security: He is willing to accept that 'the money would be tighter for a couple months' rather than take the more stable arrangement.
rationales.conformity: He pushes back on 'bodies in the building' and challenges the company's preferred arrangement instead of accommodating it.

rationales.self_direction: He immediately reaches out for the fully remote fintech project, acting to preserve the work style he wants rather than waiting to be boxed in.
rationales.security: He calmly considers a move that would 'lose about fifteen percent' of monthly income, showing willingness to trade financial comfort for other priorities.

rationales.self_direction: He says he 'cannot make myself take the easier path when it means someone else deciding how I spend my days,' making autonomy non-negotiable.
rationales.security: Even with school fees, 'we do not have a fat cushion,' and an easier, safer option available, he still leans toward the riskier path.

rationales.self_direction: He turns down the contract because the on-site requirement 'does not fit how I operate,' choosing the setup he needs over the better-paid option.
rationales.security: He accepts the fintech project 'at a slightly lower rate' because keeping control over his work matters more than maximizing stability.
rationales.benevolence: He sits down with Nadia, acknowledges 'the numbers are tight,' and treats the family's concerns seriously instead of brushing them aside.

rationales.self_direction: He chooses a half-day for himself and says he would rather 'actually deliver, then hav[e] a life outside,' asserting his own priorities over office culture.
rationales.hedonism: The whole point of the day is pleasure: 'twelve courses,' laughter with Jun, and feeling 'genuinely recharged' after the meal.
rationales.conformity: He refuses the norm of looking busy for appearances and says 'my manager will survive,' directly pushing against workplace expectations.
rationales.benevolence: He makes space for a long-awaited experience with his girlfriend, treating shared enjoyment and the relationship as worth protecting.

rationales.self_direction: He insists 'I work differently' and wants a life where he can be good at work while still keeping time and energy for what matters to him.
rationales.hedonism: He explicitly rejects a life of grinding like his uncle and says he wants to have 'energy left for everything else,' defending enjoyment and quality of life.
rationales.conformity: He does not accept his father's standard that seriousness means 'staying late' and sacrificing for the company.
rationales.tradition: He pushes back on the inherited family model of work sacrifice 'the way he did at my age,' refusing to reproduce that script as his own.

rationales.benevolence: She immediately tells Lisa "I'd come up this weekend to give her a break" and frames the degree as painful because "the people who need me are somewhere else," showing strong concern for close family.
She spends the visit doing "Mom's laundry," sitting with her so Lisa can rest, and leaving "enough in Lisa's freezer for a week," clearly centering care for her mother and sister.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects external pressure from her advisor, saying "Some things are more important than a Zoom call" and "I'm not going to apologize," which shows acting from her own priorities rather than institutional expectations.
rationales.benevolence: She missed the meeting because she was "taking care of my mother," directly prioritizing a close family member's needs.
Her focus is on Tyler's stress and how to support him well, including the hard choice to "step back" so he can handle uncertainty himself instead of trying to control it for him.

She recognizes that she has long felt "most yourself when you're giving to someone else" and recalls making lunches for Lisa in fourth grade, showing a deeply ingrained pattern of caring for close others.

rationales.achievement: After deciding to "actually be productive," she finishes coursework, confirms practicum hours, and organizes multiple responsibilities, showing deliberate task completion.
rationales.security: She calls the memory care coordinator, considers the assisted living opening, checks insurance changes, and says making a list helps her feel calmer, all of which reflect active management of stability and risk.
rationales.benevolence: Much of her to-do list is organized around supporting family, including planning for Mom's care, helping Lisa, and handling Tyler's FAFSA.
Her immediate concern is that Lisa "can't afford" to leave work and that she "should be the one doing this," showing strong responsibility toward her mother and sister.

rationales.security: She and Lisa "made a plan," scheduled visits every other weekend, and agreed to tour assisted living and consult the doctor, showing a concrete effort to create a more stable care arrangement.
rationales.benevolence: The conversation centers on sharing the burden with Lisa, supporting Mom, and honestly guiding Tyler through the family situation.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects being defined by her classmate's mockery and insists "I'm here because I've lived this. That counts for something," affirming her own internally chosen reasons for the work.

rationales.security: Touring the assisted living wing, evaluating the staff and room, and planning to discuss it with Mom's doctor show a careful move toward a safer, more sustainable arrangement.
rationales.benevolence: She brings Tyler to visit Mom, coordinates the tour with Lisa, and returns home to continue caring for the household through lunch prep and other ordinary tasks.

rationales.self_direction: She refuses the relocation and says "I'm not ready to leave yet," continuing a difficult path because it is her own choice rather than the sensible option others might prefer.
rationales.achievement: She says finishing would feel like "proof that it was worth it," showing a strong drive to make the years of work count as a real accomplishment.
rationales.security: She turns away from a "good salary, housing allowance, school fees covered" while dwelling on depleted savings and uncertainty, which clearly deprioritizes stability.

rationales.achievement: The drawing says "Amma working hard," and she keeps it by her monitor because "some days that's enough to keep going," showing motivation through effort and persistence in her work.

rationales.self_direction: She closes the email and chooses the uncertain PhD path because "the struggle is mine," explicitly favoring a life she authors herself over "someone else's version of my life."
rationales.achievement: Even after imagining the safer job, she "went back to my data," indicating continued commitment to the demanding long-term goal she has been pursuing.
rationales.security: She walks away from a "Permanent role, government pension" despite saying savings are basically gone and her husband is tired of being the only earner, which is a clear move away from stability.
rationales.conformity: She resists strong family and social expectations, including what her parents and in-laws want, rather than choosing the respectable path that would end their criticism.

rationales.self_direction: She tells both her mother and Arjun that "my timeline is when I finish," holding to her own chosen path instead of adopting the schedule others are pressing on her.
rationales.achievement: She refuses to abandon "something I've worked four years on," showing continued commitment to completing a demanding goal despite fatigue.
rationales.security: She acknowledges that her finish date is "a hope" rather than a secure plan and still rejects the more stable alternative, so predictability is clearly being sacrificed.
rationales.conformity: She pushes back against pressure from both her family and spouse rather than aligning with their expectations about when she should stop or settle.

rationales.self_direction: She reaffirms a path that "doesn't make sense on paper" because it is "work you've chosen," showing commitment to an internally chosen direction.
rationales.achievement: Praise for the chapter and the possibility of presenting at a conference matter because they signal competence and progress toward professional success.
rationales.conformity: She notes that "every aunty and uncle back home thinks I've lost the plot" yet still embraces the path, indicating willingness to go against community expectations.

rationales.stimulation: He is "teaching myself upholstery techniques that would take most people years to master," which shows active pursuit of challenge and novelty rather than settling into routine.
rationales.achievement: He wants to "list all the things I've done," highlights new orders, and feels the loss of "keeping score," showing a strong orientation toward accomplishment and recognition.

rationales.self_direction: He is independently redesigning his workshop and says he wants to take on challenges he "can't even attempt right now," showing self-directed shaping of his own work path.
rationales.stimulation: The most satisfying part was the difficult technical problem of matching the veneer after "three tries" and hours of research, and he is actively seeking even bigger restoration challenges next.
rationales.achievement: He invested "forty hours" in the restoration, tracked the result with photos, and took satisfaction in knowing "it was good work before she saw it," reflecting a clear drive for mastery and excellence.
rationales.tradition: He researched "1920s Danish furniture styles" and carefully matched the original veneer grain, showing respect for preserving inherited craftsmanship and historical continuity.

rationales.hedonism: She says yes to Korean BBQ but is already thinking about "how much marking I need to clear," letting work crowd out anticipated enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: She stayed until "7pm revising the slides" after feedback about pacing, showing extra effort to improve her teaching performance.
She keeps investing heavy effort in lessons, noting she is still "cutting out laminated shapes at 6:30" and plans to improve pacing next term.
rationales.conformity: She downplays her hurt, frames Mrs. Tan's comment as normal, and decides to "focus on pacing next term," adapting herself to an experienced mentor's expectations.
rationales.benevolence: Her choices are driven by students' experience, and Aisha saying math became her favorite because of the "fraction pizza activity" shows she is prioritizing the children's engagement.

rationales.hedonism: She cancels the Christmas market, a birthday dinner, and a spa day because work keeps taking over, explicitly sacrificing pleasure and rest.
rationales.achievement: She "insisted on writing personalized comments for all 32 students" and worked until 9pm, choosing higher-effort excellence over convenience.
rationales.benevolence: Writing individualized reports for every student and cherishing Dinesh's "Best Teacher" card show sustained care for the children in her class.

rationales.stimulation: She leans into novelty and movement by going to a night market, buying silly keychains, and planning a day trip to Malacca for a famous food spot.
rationales.hedonism: The entry centers on savoring food, "lazed around," reading "an actual novel," and noticing relief from stress once she steps away from work.

rationales.self_direction: She consciously resists the anxious urge to check email and decides, "I'm going to close the laptop. Tomorrow is soon enough," choosing her own boundary over internal pressure.
rationales.hedonism: She wants to "protect this feeling a little longer" and closes the laptop to preserve rest, presence, and enjoyment rather than re-enter work stress.

rationales.self_direction: She pushes back on the prescribed career ladder, asking "Why can't I just be good at teaching?" and defining success around classroom work rather than the promoted path.
rationales.benevolence: What matters most to her is that "Dinesh... is finally starting to participate in class," showing her focus on the growth of the children she teaches.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects external metrics, saying the point is "not for reviews or promotions" but for what feels true in the classroom.
rationales.benevolence: She notices Aisha's story, praises her privately, and tells her mother about it, actively nurturing the child's confidence and creativity.

rationales.hedonism: Even during a nice brunch and walk, she "couldn't stop calculating how many hours until Monday," showing that leisure is being overridden by anticipatory work stress.

rationales.tradition: She keeps the novena "circled on my calendar since January" and teaches her students the annual altar practice, treating repetition as a meaningful way to keep family and ritual alive.

rationales.self_direction: She says she wants "space to figure that out" but is already concealing the relationship because of anticipated family judgment, letting others' expectations override her own process.
rationales.security: She avoids telling the truth because she anticipates painful "gentle questions that feel like tiny knives," choosing temporary safety over openness.
rationales.conformity: Her secrecy is driven by pressure from a family that "still whispers about my cousin's civil ceremony," showing restraint to avoid social disapproval.
rationales.tradition: The conflict is framed through family and religious expectations around marriage, especially her fear of bringing "a divorced man" into that setting.
rationales.benevolence: Carlos "looked hurt" because she lied about why he could not meet her family, and she recognizes she is deceiving him while protecting herself.

rationales.tradition: She is helping prepare for "tomorrow's final night of the novena" and notes she has set up chairs "every year since I was fourteen," showing continuity with a longstanding ritual.
rationales.benevolence: She spends the evening cooking with family, caring for a gathering centered on relatives and shared prayer, and helping make the event possible.

rationales.self_direction: She says she stayed quiet in the staff meeting even though she had clear objections, suppressing her own view because speaking honestly felt too exposed.
rationales.conformity: When asked what she thought, she said "it sounded fine" rather than voicing disagreement, smoothing over conflict instead of challenging the group.
rationales.tradition: She explicitly defends older methods, ritual, and the continuity represented by Sister Magdalena and the physical practice of books and pencils.
She rejects the idea that staying means being stuck, grounding herself in family and place by saying "The roots here are mine."
rationales.benevolence: She stays because "This is where I'm needed," naming her grandmother and students as reasons to remain close and available to the people who depend on her.

rationales.self_direction: She says, "I don't know how to write a different one and still feel like myself," showing how inherited expectations are constraining her ability to choose her own path.
rationales.conformity: She is preoccupied with keeping everyone comfortable and fears becoming "the version of me who disappoints people," which pulls her toward restraint and social approval.
rationales.tradition: Her conflict is measured against "the script" of her parents' church-centered marriage and the family model she was raised to follow.

rationales.security: After confession she sits in the pew where her grandmother always sits, and the fact that the church "smelled the same way it did when I was seven" brings her a sense of comfort and steadiness.
rationales.tradition: The entry is anchored in confession, Father Miguel's guidance, and the reassurance of practices and places that have structured her life since childhood.
She keeps the same "San Francisco de Asis poster" her mother gave her in her first year and decides not to replace it because "Some things are allowed to show their age."

rationales.self_direction: She defines the needed break as "Telling the full truth and accepting whatever comes after," choosing an authentic life over compartmentalized performance.
rationales.security: She becomes willing to face "a difficult Sunday dinner" and family disapproval rather than keep arranging everything around what feels safe.
rationales.conformity: She explicitly rejects "performing okay-ness" and no longer wants to keep separate worlds apart just to manage family comfort.
rationales.benevolence: She says "Carlos deserves better than being my secret," showing concern for the harm her secrecy is causing someone close to her.

rationales.self_direction: Despite shaking, she brings Carlos to Sunday lunch and concludes that she must "stop hiding the pieces that don't" fit, acting from honesty rather than concealment.
rationales.security: She steps into a tense, uncertain family situation with "silences that stretched too long," giving up the safety of secrecy.
rationales.conformity: She accepts that bringing her relationship into the open "doesn't mean it fits perfectly," choosing truth over smooth adherence to family expectations.
rationales.tradition: The relationship is intentionally brought into "Sunday lunch at my parents' house," and she frames the moment as bringing her life to the tradition honestly rather than abandoning it.
rationales.benevolence: She integrates Carlos into family life and the scene centers on care from close others, including her grandmother holding his hand and her mother acknowledging "he's good to you."

rationales.hedonism: She deliberately chose to "stay horizontal and feel the sun on my face" and savored a day with waves, sun, and laughter as needed relief.
She centers sensory pleasure in "the tahdig was crispy and perfect" and says her stomach hurts "in the best way possible," making enjoyment the point of the evening.
rationales.benevolence: She says what made the night special was "good food, good company," showing genuine value placed on time with close friends.

rationales.self_direction: She resists family pressure by saying she wants to "rest without having to justify my choices to anyone," asserting her right to choose her own life.
rationales.conformity: She explicitly recoils from gatherings where relatives will question her life choices, showing refusal to accommodate expected social scripts.
rationales.tradition: The family pressure around marriage and the complaint about "Lebanese aunties being Lebanese aunties" shows active distance from customary expectations.
rationales.benevolence: She spent hours "holding her mother's hand while the doctors explained options," which is direct care for someone in distress.

rationales.self_direction: She defends her right to live on her own terms, insisting she will not "justify" a life that includes caring for herself rather than total work sacrifice.
rationales.hedonism: She argues that "taking care of myself" matters and vows not to let a job "steal my life," clearly prioritizing enjoyment and life outside work.
rationales.security: Her father's heart attack taught her to protect her own wellbeing, and she frames overwork as a threat she is determined to avoid.
rationales.conformity: She rejects the workplace norm that worth comes from endless sacrifice, pushing back on Fatima's judgment about how she should live.
rationales.benevolence: She gives concrete examples of caring behavior, like staying late to comfort a frightened child and holding a mother's hand for hours.

rationales.stimulation: Booking a trip to Muscat and anticipating a resort getaway shows active pursuit of novelty and a change of scene.
rationales.hedonism: She is excited about the "infinity pool overlooking the Gulf" and says she has been "counting down the days," making anticipated pleasure central.
She insists that "Rest is doing something" and describes a weekend built around poolside reading, room service, and sunset watching.
rationales.benevolence: She wants Sara to release her guilt because "I see myself in her," and frames helping her friend avoid burnout as something that matters.

rationales.self_direction: She is clear that the life her family praises "doesn't sound like the life I want," affirming her own path over others' expectations.
rationales.conformity: She sees family comparison and marriage pressure as something they "don't understand," showing resistance to complying with expected roles.
rationales.tradition: The entry directly rejects a conventional marriage-and-family script, including being introduced to a spouse and quitting work to "start a family."

rationales.hedonism: She says she measures life by "good meals, beautiful sunsets, moments of genuine laughter," explicitly centering enjoyment and aliveness.

rationales.benevolence: She cannot help getting attached to the child, describing small acts like "adjusting her pillows" and bringing "extra apple juice," which shows personal care and tenderness.

rationales.hedonism: She celebrates three days off with "sleeping in and maybe getting a massage" and says "That's enough," explicitly valuing comfort and pleasure.

rationales.benevolence: His salary worries are tied to caring for family, especially Amma's worsening knees and Priya's coming expenses.

rationales.security: He frames the dilemma around whether he can afford Amma's surgery, treating financial adequacy as a pressing need.

rationales.security: The possible change feels threatening enough that he "could not sleep" and says acting on it would unsettle "Everything," showing a strong need for continuity and stability.

rationales.power: He frames Nadia's film-school plan as "dismantling the one plan I had for this family's future" and sees himself as the one who must "hold the center."
rationales.tradition: He centers Mama's table, being "the eldest," and repeatedly choosing the role that keeps the family structure intact.
rationales.benevolence: He describes himself as "the anchor" who stays to "keep things together here," showing active responsibility for close family members.
Beyond work problems, he spends Thursday driving to Irbid because "Mama's water heater broke," prioritizing practical help for family despite exhaustion.

rationales.tradition: Rania uses "Mama's recipe" and he reflects that "some things do carry forward without anyone forcing them," explicitly valuing inherited practices continuing over time.

rationales.power: He says yes to Samir's proposal and tells Nadia "fine without really thinking about it," then wonders whether these are merely "concessions," showing a loosening of control rather than asserting it.

rationales.benevolence: He listens to Nadia talk "through dinner" and feels "pride," showing warm, supportive investment in his daughter's excitement.

rationales.security: He feels a "weight in my chest every time I think about the company ten years from now with no family in it," showing anxiety about long-term stability.

rationales.self_direction: He finally acts by "stop[ping] waiting for the feeling to go away" and making a difficult choice despite discomfort, showing deliberate personal agency.

rationales.benevolence: He allows the application after seeing Nadia cry and the whole house feel "lighter," prioritizing her wellbeing over his preferred plan.

rationales.hedonism: She lingers on the pleasure of "coffee the way I like it, with cardamom," fruit on the balcony, and "No agenda," treating unstructured enjoyment as what made those mornings precious.
She chooses "leftover knafeh for breakfast" and a cooking show for two hours, trying to enjoy herself in the empty house.
rationales.conformity: She knows she "should be doing something productive" but ignores that inner policing voice, resisting the duty-bound standard she associates with her mother.

rationales.hedonism: She deliberately savors "Twenty minutes of nothing" with cardamom coffee on the balcony and experiences it as reclaiming a small pleasure.
She sees Saturday tutoring as giving up "the one day we have no obligations," so the outcome threatens her only open time for ease and enjoyment.
rationales.conformity: She does not say no because she expects a lecture about "what a good mother would do," showing deference to family expectations and pressure.

rationales.hedonism: After a draining day, "the best part" is a bath "so hot it turned my skin red," with the entry anchored in craving bodily relief and comfort.
rationales.conformity: She spends the visit "smiling, helping in the kitchen, making sure the children behave" and agreeing with relatives, carefully performing the expected family role.

rationales.hedonism: She treasures "a warm evening and good ice cream" where she could enjoy two scoops and "didn't think about anything else."
rationales.benevolence: The scene centers on shared joy with close family, especially "my kids being happy" and Tariq naming the outing as what matters.

rationales.self_direction: She asserts "I don't need to justify it" after buying the candle and book, explicitly claiming the right to choose for herself without external approval.
rationales.hedonism: The purchase is for immediate sensory and personal enjoyment, capped by "The candle smells incredible though."

rationales.self_direction: She says every open space gets "assigned a purpose by someone else" and worries she is "disappearing entirely into the role," showing loss of personal choice.
rationales.hedonism: She mourns the loss of "something unstructured, something that just feels good," so the tutoring arrangement directly crowds out pleasure and rest.
rationales.conformity: The family's approving group-chat response and her fear of sounding like she "don't care about my children's upbringing" show strong pressure to comply with maternal expectations.

rationales.hedonism: She describes eating "cold rice standing at the counter" after staying late and taking Sofia's call, showing present comfort was repeatedly sacrificed.
rationales.achievement: She "stayed late yesterday organizing what we do have, trying to stretch twenty copies across thirty-two desks," showing determined effort to make the classroom work despite shortages.
rationales.benevolence: She helped Mama with exercises and spent "two hours" listening to Sofia cry even though she was exhausted, prioritizing close others' needs over her own.

rationales.security: She feels relief in "She's here. She's okay... right now, in this house, we're together," grounding herself in safety and family stability.
rationales.tradition: Mama's soft "buenas noches, mija" functions like a familiar family ritual, and the moment's comfort comes from that continuity.
rationales.benevolence: Her strongest emotional center is being "together" with Mama and holding onto that bond when everything else feels overwhelming.

rationales.self_direction: She resists outside judgment about "why I didn't take the department head job" and insists, "I made the right choice," signaling an autonomous decision.
rationales.power: Declining "the department head job" is a direct refusal of added status and authority.
rationales.benevolence: Her justification turns on "no one depending on her," implying she chose against advancement because of responsibility to people who rely on her.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects others' expectations with "This is why I'm here. Not running meetings about assessment metrics," choosing the kind of work that fits her own priorities.
rationales.achievement: She takes pride in a concrete success: Andres "raised his hand and told everyone" about his grandmother's farm after once barely speaking.
rationales.power: "Not running meetings about assessment metrics" frames leadership and administrative authority as exactly what she does not want.
rationales.tradition: Mama made empanadas to mark the first day, and the celebration is rooted in a familiar family custom rather than novelty.
rationales.benevolence: The whole entry centers on caring about a child's growth, especially "watching a kid find his voice" and remembering his mother's earlier worry.
She lights up because Sofia "didn't ask for anything, just wanted to share," and she immediately wonders whether she helped her get there.

rationales.universalism: He volunteers to refurbish laptops for families and gives his email to a teenager who wants to learn programming, framing the work around helping people who need access.
After helping Ahmad, he reads about "e-waste dumping in Ghana" and judges the laptop project against a larger global harm, even while deciding it is "still worth doing."

rationales.benevolence: He lingers over Ahmad's first webpage and feels moved by the teenager's progress, showing genuine personal investment in someone he is mentoring.

rationales.universalism: He cares that the route module means "fewer emissions per delivery," showing concern for environmental impact even though he keeps it to himself at work.

rationales.benevolence: He tells Elsa "congratulations and meant it" and stays focused on being proud of her rather than burdening her with his critique.
rationales.universalism: His unease centers on what her firm's models do "at scale" for fast fashion supply chains, showing concern with systemic environmental and human consequences beyond his immediate circle.

rationales.achievement: He marks a concrete accomplishment by pushing the feature to staging and quantifying that the fleet saves "roughly 14% on redundant kilometres per week."
rationales.universalism: He privately calculates the "carbon maths" and treats the emissions reduction as morally meaningful, not just a business metric.

rationales.conformity: He stays silent at the table because speaking would cost "A scene. And Margareta's peace. And maybe Elsa's evening," showing restraint to preserve social harmony.
rationales.tradition: The entry is set around a midsummer gathering, and he is participating in that familiar seasonal ritual with family food and late-evening light.
rationales.universalism: He wants to push back because "the Baltic is choking on microplastics," showing that environmental harm is the issue he cannot stop tracking.

rationales.conformity: He imagines speaking up but stops because he would be "the difficult one again" who makes gatherings tense, showing strong pressure to maintain social smoothness.

rationales.universalism: He justifies staying with the route module partly because "it does reduce emissions," keeping wider social and environmental effects in view.

rationales.benevolence: He keeps checking his phone because Ahmad's hearing is next week and still plans to help one family through the laptop group, showing concrete care for particular people.
rationales.universalism: The entry is saturated with distress over refugees drowning in the Mediterranean and the contrast between "water as recreation versus water as a death sentence."

rationales.benevolence: He stays emotionally engaged with Ahmad's hearing and keeps returning to the laptop group because "Sometimes the small thing is the only honest thing you can do."
rationales.universalism: He reflects on container ships, waste, climate hypocrisy, and systemic failure, showing persistent concern for environmental and social harms far beyond his own immediate life.

rationales.conformity: He resists his family's narrow idea that 'the only version of success they understand has a degree attached to it,' rather than aligning with that expectation.

rationales.power: He admits he may 'just can't stand not being in the middle of it,' which points to a strong need to stay central and in control.
rationales.benevolence: He let Sami take Friday off 'for a family thing' even though it disrupted operations and made more work for him.

rationales.achievement: He successfully closed the restaurant deal because 'my prices were better than the other guy's,' showing focus on competence and results.
rationales.power: What stings most is not just the deal but that he was not treated like 'the one making decisions,' showing concern with status and authority.

rationales.hedonism: He turned down football with Khaled and stayed glued to work even though 'the routes were fine,' sacrificing enjoyment for control.

rationales.achievement: He experiences the resignation as a blow to 'what I built,' showing how strongly he identifies with building and maintaining something successful.

rationales.self_direction: She values that the owner wanting 'something colorful' gives her 'so much room to play,' emphasizing creative freedom in how she works.

rationales.benevolence: She frames the trip around 'people I love' and values time with 'the people who matter,' centering close relationships.

rationales.security: She rejects the cheaper flatshare because the lease is 'month-to-month,' the building lacks 'proper insurance,' and she worries others may not 'pay on time.' Her condition for saying yes is 'a proper lease' with protections if someone leaves, showing clear prioritization of stability over savings.

rationales.achievement: She celebrates getting into the 'autumn group show' and says she has 'been working toward this since January.' Being selected without having to explain her work signals recognized competence and progress toward a clear artistic goal.

rationales.security: She spends time fixing the savings transfer so 'the auto-transfer is set for the 2nd of every month again' and the 'buffer is back where it should be.' Her explanation about not wanting to 'end up stuck' and remembering when money was 'genuinely scary' shows active effort to preserve financial stability.

rationales.hedonism: She keeps working until her 'eyes are burning' after restarting the panel twice, then settles for 'leftovers and sleep.' The entry shows pleasure and comfort being deprioritized in favor of finishing the piece.
rationales.achievement: She pushes through repeated mistakes on the 'second piece for the Moreau show' and starts the panel over twice to get it right. That persistence under fatigue reflects strong commitment to producing high-quality work.

rationales.security: She says the frame shop 'works for me' because 'it's predictable' and 'having those three shifts is like having a floor under me.' She explicitly values the steady rhythm and income because it makes the rest of her work feel less chaotic.

rationales.stimulation: She is irritated by Hugo freelancing with 'no savings, no backup plan' and rejects the idea that this kind of uncertainty is 'brave.' The entry clearly pushes against risk and novelty-seeking rather than embracing them.
rationales.security: She defends having 'rent covered through March' and insists that 'keeping something steady is' not the same as 'not trying.' Her focus is on maintaining a dependable safety net while pursuing art.

She repeatedly chooses dependable arrangements: moving money into savings, tracking a 'year-end target,' and keeping the phone plan that 'just works' despite the higher cost. In the response she sums it up as paying for 'safe choices' and admits taking the risk makes her 'chest get tight,' showing a clear preference for certainty.

As soon as Girard mentions he 'might cut hours,' she starts planning for 'if he cuts my shifts to two, or one, or none.' The immediate focus on downside scenarios and contingency thinking shows strong concern with protecting stability.

rationales.hedonism: He eats biryani 'standing at my desk between Slack messages' during a day of nonstop calls. Even a potentially enjoyable meal is treated as something to squeeze in while working, rather than savoring comfort or pleasure.

rationales.self_direction: He says, 'I didn't want to execute someone else's vision forever. I wanted the room to look at me and know that I'm the one making the call.' That directly frames his career choice around autonomy and authoring the direction himself.
rationales.achievement: He takes pride in laying out the 'Q3 roadmap,' handling questions cleanly, and hearing that his enterprise pivot 'made sense' after months of skepticism. The entry centers on competence being demonstrated and recognized by others.
rationales.power: He relishes that the team 'went quiet when I started talking' because what he said 'mattered' and that 'I'm the one making the call.' The strongest signal here is satisfaction from authority, influence, and being seen as the person steering the company.

When Vikram 'cut me off mid-pitch,' the investor 'looked at him, not me, for the rest of the call,' and he 'didn't say anything.' The moment is defined by losing control of the room and ceding authority in a high-stakes interaction.

He is consumed by the fact that 'the entire Q3 plan hinges on this one green light' from procurement and says, 'I'm the CEO of this company and I can't move my own roadmap forward.' The entry centers on frustration with being unable to exert control or influence outcomes.
rationales.conformity: He says each follow-up email is 'carefully worded' because he cannot sound 'desperate' or 'pushy' and has to project the right tone. That deliberate self-restraint to fit professional expectations is a clear signal of norm-sensitive behavior.

rationales.power: What lingers is 'the moment I saw the room shift' and 'watching them choose someone else's direction over mine.' The strongest signal is his distress at losing authority and having others follow a rival viewpoint inside the company he built.
rationales.conformity: He says he 'agreed to his approach because fighting it further would have made me look like I was digging in out of ego.' He restrains his impulse to keep pushing in order to manage how he is perceived and preserve group dynamics.

rationales.power: A younger founder dismisses him with 'oh, still early then,' and he is rattled by feeling like 'a booth he'd already walked past.' The hurt comes from status threat and not being accorded the respect or standing he believes he has earned.

rationales.achievement: He says what 'recharges' him is 'closing a deal, seeing our revenue numbers tick up, watching someone I hired three years ago become a team lead.' Those are explicit markers of success, growth, and demonstrated competence.
rationales.power: He overrides routing decisions for 'optics,' emphasizes that 'names matter,' strategically sits next to a deputy minister, and attends dinners to track competitors. The entry consistently shows him pursuing influence, status, and control through relationships and perception.

rationales.hedonism: He dismisses the idea that work should be enjoyable, writing, 'Not everything has to recharge you - that is a luxury mindset.' That directly rejects pleasure and emotional replenishment as valid priorities.

rationales.achievement: He chooses the outside candidate because 'good is not enough for where we are going' and frames the decision around whether they become 'a regional leader or another footnote.' The choice is driven by scaling, excellence, and winning rather than maintaining comfort.
rationales.power: He wants someone who 'walks into a room and commands it' and insists sentiment cannot override the decisions that determine their market position. The entry emphasizes influence, authority, and selecting for commanding presence.
rationales.benevolence: He knowingly overrides the case for promoting Hana, even after Youssef argues that 'loyalty should count for something.' Although he offers her a role and bonus, he still subordinates the interests of a longtime insider to strategic advantage.
He immediately let Jenna leave to help her locked-out roommate and then "spent the last hour doing her side work plus mine," putting her stress and safety ahead of his own convenience.

He noticed Tommy was "off," asked what was wrong, and sent him home after hearing about his grandfather's death, choosing to cover the shift himself. The line "Sometimes you just do the thing you'd want someone to do for you" shows deliberate care for someone nearby.

He had "sent three emails and sat in a forty-five minute call with regional" to try to get Priya promoted, and he keeps replaying the conversation because he feels he let her down. The entry centers on concern for how the decision affected her, not just himself.

rationales.self_direction: He pushes back on the district manager's stance by insisting "my team has lives outside of work," asserting his own judgment instead of simply accepting top-down control.
rationales.conformity: He openly resists the overtime crackdown after being told he has approved "too many shift swaps," rather than falling in line with the expectation to prioritize rules over employees' needs.
rationales.benevolence: His defense of shift swaps is explicitly grounded in concern for coworkers' lives outside work, showing active care for the people he manages.

He is moved less by the coffee itself than by the fact that Jenna remembered, concluding that the job is sometimes "just about people noticing each other." That response reflects valuing reciprocal care and thoughtfulness in close relationships.

rationales.hedonism: He keeps saying yes to others in ways that cost him "meals I skip" and "sleep I don't get," showing repeated self-denial rather than protecting his own comfort or enjoyment.
rationales.security: He stayed because "I wasn't going to make her walk home in the dark" and "It's not safe," making protection from harm the deciding factor.
rationales.benevolence: He waited an extra hour with Maria, listened to her talk about her mother's chemo and bills, and frames his struggle around not wanting to fail people who need him.
He says "that's why I do it" after hearing Priya stayed because "of how we treat people here," showing that making coworkers feel "seen" and valued is his main motivation.

rationales.universalism: She spent "almost an hour" helping Fatima learn to write her own name and treats the exhausting Saturday trip as worthwhile because it expands access to literacy beyond her immediate circle. The focus is on broader human welfare, not personal gain.

She is encouraged that a lesson about "water pollution" reached a student's family and calls that "a small thread pulling in the right direction." The entry shows commitment to wider social and environmental wellbeing beyond her classroom.

rationales.benevolence: After finishing the attendance work, she "stayed back to help the peon uncle fix a broken desk," giving extra time to support someone in her immediate work community.

rationales.conformity: She "kept quiet because Sharma sir is senior" and did not challenge him in the meeting, restraining her impulse to avoid disrupting the hierarchy.
rationales.universalism: She rejects the claim that poor parents do not care about education, pointing to families working "twelve-hour shifts" and Ravi's mother walking "forty minutes" to attend. Her concern is clearly with fairness and respect for disadvantaged families beyond her personal circle.

She knowingly chose the convenient chain store even though the local women's group "sell at lower prices and the money goes directly back to the community," then calls the choice hypocritical. The session describes failing to act on her own fairness-oriented principles in a concrete decision.

rationales.self_direction: When Baba and Didi push her toward coaching and a "better school," she answers "I like my school" and insists that being "okay right now" matters, choosing her own path over others' ambitions for her.
rationales.universalism: She uses her unexpected free day to plan a unit on "water and sanitation" because students carry those conversations home, showing concern for broader public welfare through her teaching.

rationales.hedonism: She cancels the dinner she had been anticipating at the "new coastal restaurant" and ends up eating a quick street snack after a draining shift, with pleasure clearly sacrificed to duty.
rationales.security: She says "I don't like the uncertainty" about a possible shift rotation because "The current schedule works for me," showing a strong preference for stability and predictability.
rationales.tradition: Her decision is guided by her mother's teaching: "when someone is drowning in grief, you throw them a rope, even if your arms are tired," explicitly grounding her response in a family moral lesson.
rationales.benevolence: She stayed an extra forty-five minutes because "The family was alone" and "leaving felt wrong," prioritizing direct care for distressed people in front of her despite exhaustion.

rationales.stimulation: She reacts negatively to the abrupt move to night shifts after "five years of day shifts," describing the upheaval as exhausting and unwelcome rather than energizing.
rationales.hedonism: Her planned Goa trip was about sitting "on the beach and eat fish curry and do absolutely nothing productive for three days," showing an explicit investment in rest and enjoyment.
rationales.security: The entry emphasizes how she had spent "five years of building my life around that schedule" and is upset by learning of the change through WhatsApp, signaling that predictable routine matters deeply to her.

rationales.self_direction: She insists that "my life outside these walls is mine to plan," and directly argues for control over her schedule rather than simply accepting management's decision.
rationales.stimulation: She repeatedly rejects upheaval, asking for "predictability" and describing her life being rearranged by "someone else's spreadsheet" as deeply frustrating.
rationales.hedonism: Keeping the Goa trip required a costly swap that means she has "already traded away a holiday," showing leisure and comfort being sacrificed rather than protected.
rationales.security: She centers the need for stability, citing promised "schedule stability," the effect on her health, and the plea "I just want some predictability."
rationales.conformity: Even while upset, she says "I didn't raise my voice" and emphasizes "I work hard. I show up. I don't complain," reflecting restraint and a continued effort to meet expectations.

rationales.achievement: He immediately called the CFO for numbers and focused on how to handle the renegotiation effectively, showing strong concern with business performance and competence.
rationales.power: He is fixated on not being cornered publicly and says, "You don't ambush the person who signs your contracts," emphasizing authority and control over the situation.
rationales.conformity: He objects less to the terms than to Minh violating the expected hierarchy by staging the request publicly, and wants to reassert his dominance rather than preserve mutual accommodation.
rationales.benevolence: He weighs the office as "profitable but not indispensable" and frames the issue around preserving his authority, with little concern for the partner's needs or relationship strain.

rationales.self_direction: He independently decides to "book the flight" and handle the situation on his own terms rather than accept how it was framed for him.
rationales.power: "Didn't mention the revenue share. Let him wonder" shows deliberate use of uncertainty and in-person presence to regain leverage and control.
rationales.conformity: Instead of transparent, norm-following communication, he withholds his real agenda to unsettle Minh and manage the interaction strategically.
rationales.benevolence: His choice to "let him wonder" prioritizes pressure and advantage over a considerate or supportive approach to the other person.

rationales.achievement: He treats the meeting as a successful performance, getting Minh down from twelve to five percent and preserving profitability.
rationales.power: "I steered the conversation" and "Minh will do what I need him to do" make control and dominance the clearest signal in the entry.
rationales.security: He explicitly values that "the office stays profitable" and that the relationship remains "technically intact," showing preference for stability and risk containment.
rationales.conformity: He preserves formal harmony by shaking hands publicly and taking everyone to dinner, managing appearances and social order even while exerting control.
rationales.benevolence: He recognizes Minh "went along with it because he had to," yet still counts it as a win, indicating willingness to damage trust for his own advantage.

rationales.achievement: Being sought out to mentor a Series B pitch because he "doesn't sugarcoat" signals recognized competence and success.
rationales.power: He enjoys being "the one they came to," which reflects status and influence within his network.
rationales.benevolence: Agreeing to mentor younger founders shows active investment in helping people in his professional circle.

rationales.achievement: He defends the school choice because of "the name and the network" and the doors it will open, showing strong emphasis on advancement and success.
rationales.power: He frames the decision around building a future where access and elite credentials create leverage he never had.
rationales.security: "The whole point of the school... is so she never has to struggle the way I did" directly ties the choice to protection and a more secure future.
rationales.benevolence: He admits the decision may have been "about my idea of what her life should look like, not hers," and accepts being treated like "a service provider," showing a controlling rather than attuned stance toward his daughter.
He reaches out with a personal photo of the dog and lingers over her small response, showing care for rebuilding closeness with his daughter.

rationales.hedonism: "Twelve-hour days," running on "instant coffee and sheer stubbornness," and being too exhausted even to turn on the TV show clear self-denial rather than enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: Closing the Series C extension after intense effort and wanting someone to say he "did well" reflects strong striving for excellence and recognition.
rationales.power: He emphasizes holding "the line on the terms I wanted" in a high-stakes financing process, showing assertive control over outcomes.
rationales.security: Securing the funding round reduces pressure and provides organizational stability, which is why he feels primarily "relief."
rationales.benevolence: The entry centers on the deal and his recognition, with overwork leaving little room for attending to others beyond perfunctory thanks.

rationales.achievement: He is energized by exceeding expectations, being noticed by the board, and seeing it as the path to the "regional director track."
rationales.power: He withholds credit because he does not want to "make myself look like I need hand-holding from my own team," prioritizing status and authority.
rationales.benevolence: He knowingly omits Ahmed's contribution to protect his own advancement, despite admitting the idea was originally Ahmed's.

rationales.hedonism: "Pool day," "Nobu," and a "recovery brunch" are explicitly framed as a pleasurable reset after work pressure.

rationales.self_direction: He says he has "never really separated" his own rule from his father's, showing deference to inherited authority scripts rather than independent judgment.
rationales.power: He is preoccupied with not losing authority, insisting "I'm not here to be liked. I'm here to deliver" and equating leadership with unquestioned control.
rationales.conformity: He tells Rami concerns should be brought "privately" and invokes a model where people "follow," showing preference for restrained behavior that preserves hierarchy.
rationales.tradition: He explicitly roots his view in family models: "the men I respected ran their households and their businesses the same way," indicating attachment to established patriarchal norms.

rationales.hedonism: He chooses an expensive dinner because he wanted "good food" and still considers putting the boat party "on the card" because "you only live once."
rationales.power: He wanted people "to see us there" and relished the team's reaction to his paying, using spending to project status and leadership.
rationales.security: He admits his account will be "tight," with the car payment due, yet considers adding more discretionary spending to the card, undermining financial stability.

rationales.power: He defends his spending as necessary because "You can't network from a studio in Sharjah," showing priority on image, status, and social positioning.
rationales.security: He dismisses his mother's advice to save for "a proper apartment," favoring visibility and networking over longer-term financial stability.
rationales.tradition: He explicitly rejects his mother's more conservative expectations around saving and proper household establishment.

rationales.hedonism: He misses the night he wanted and ends up "eating salmon rolls under fluorescent lights" instead of going out, sacrificing enjoyment to deal with work.
rationales.achievement: Staying until 10pm to repair the proposal and keep the company "still in the running" shows strong commitment to delivering results under pressure.

rationales.self_direction: He deliberately takes himself to brunch alone and considers whether "the experience is enough," moving toward relying on his own experience instead of external validation.
rationales.hedonism: The entry centers on enjoying "unlimited sparkling and eggs royale," the sun, and finally feeling relaxed.
rationales.power: He questions his need for an audience and says maybe he does not need everyone to know he was there, loosening his usual attachment to visible status and impression management.

rationales.achievement: Receiving regional recognition, a plaque, and a photo with the CEO directly reflects success and external recognition for performance.

rationales.power: He admits part of the appeal is that "She makes me look good. We look good together," treating the relationship as a status asset.
rationales.benevolence: He repeatedly cancels, avoids the difficult conversation, and wants to keep the relationship light despite knowing her needs are different.

rationales.self_direction: He is seriously considering Hassan's unperformed, self-chosen life and is disturbed by the possibility that "doing what he wants" might be enough.
rationales.power: He describes Hassan's life as having "No status, no visibility" and feels envy rather than admiration for the hierarchy itself, signaling disillusionment with his usual status metrics.

rationales.security: He values the Trane contract because "it's recurring revenue" and it covers salary through winter, emphasizing steadiness and protection from uncertainty.
rationales.benevolence: The relief is tied to "keep[ing] the lights on for the guys who depend on me," showing care for employees in his close circle.

rationales.self_direction: He resists the recruiter's offer because "I built this business from one van and a credit card" and does not want a salary path chosen for him.
"That's why I do this" and his insistence on being "the one up in that ceiling" show a strong commitment to self-authored, hands-on work.
rationales.achievement: He takes pride in solving a difficult problem others would have missed, especially when the office manager reacts like he "performed surgery."

rationales.security: He is focused on the risk that work might "slow down in October" and on avoiding an unstable hiring commitment he may not be able to sustain.
rationales.benevolence: He genuinely weighs helping Dave and worries about being "the guy who could've helped family and chose the spreadsheet instead."

rationales.hedonism: After the work is handled, he appreciates a simple pleasure: "I'm sitting on the deck with a beer and the yard looks good. That's enough for tonight."
rationales.security: "Everything got done. Everyone got paid. Truck's gassed up for tomorrow" reflects satisfaction in order, readiness, and basic stability.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the corporate role because "what I have now is smaller and harder but it's mine," emphasizing autonomy over externally defined stability.
rationales.security: He does care about having "a net," but only one he controls, showing continued concern with dependable protection against risk.

rationales.achievement: She says her "own print series hasn't moved since July" because she has become the person handling "administrative emails" and access logistics instead of advancing her own work.
rationales.conformity: She spends forty minutes mediating the press dispute and proposes an alternating schedule, prioritizing smooth group functioning over her own agenda.
rationales.benevolence: She steps in to resolve conflict between Sylvain and Oriane and helps both get a workable arrangement, supporting people in her immediate collective.

rationales.self_direction: She protects autonomous creative time by insisting that "Mornings are mine" and keeping admin from taking over the part of the day reserved for her own painting.
rationales.achievement: She completes the underpainting on a canvas she had been "circling around for weeks" and focuses on getting the work right, down to finding "the right temperature for the water."
She has been thinking about the prize "for two months" but "haven't even started" her own materials, holding herself back because she thinks her work "isn't cohesive enough."
rationales.conformity: She agrees to review a direct competitor's statement "because what else do you say," showing deference to social expectation over her own competing priorities.
rationales.benevolence: She spends time editing Oriane's statement even though they are applying to the same prize, helping someone close to her at a personal cost.

rationales.achievement: She re-evaluates her portfolio, identifies how the work connects, and breaks the application into concrete next steps like drafting the statement, choosing images, and getting better photographs before the deadline.
She turns anxiety into a concrete plan, deciding to "set the alarm for six and work on the statement before anyone else gets to the studio," which is a direct move toward completing the application.

rationales.self_direction: She immediately gives up her planned canvas-photo session because "you don't say no to Sunday lunch," letting family expectation overrule her own chosen priorities.
rationales.achievement: She follows through on the early start, opens the statement document, and works through multiple drafts to improve it rather than avoiding the task.
rationales.conformity: Her reasoning is explicitly norm-driven: she says yes to lunch "immediately" because refusing is simply not what one does in her family.

rationales.power: She realizes she keeps the coordination role partly because "I'm in charge" and "People defer to my schedule," and admits that being needed gives her a valued sense of influence and status.

rationales.conformity: She describes compensating for her "impractical path" by being "responsible about everything else," listing rule-following behaviors like paying bills on time and managing other adults' logistics to remain legible in family conversations.

rationales.achievement: She finishes the artist statement, photographs all ten pieces, and gets the application to the point where she "just need[s] to press send," showing clear progress toward an external standard of success.
She has already "Submitted the Prix Dauphine application," which is a concrete act of putting her work forward for competitive evaluation.
rationales.security: She hides the submission, throws herself into admin, and says it felt "safer to be the person who organises things than the person who wants things," explicitly choosing psychological safety over exposure.

rationales.tradition: He values that the glaze came out "exactly as Master Kwon taught me" and immediately shares the finished piece with his mother in a moment tied to inherited craft practice and family continuity.
He judges the proposal through his master's teaching, worrying that projections would add decoration the clay "did not ask for," which shows deference to established craft principles.
He joins the festival planning board because his "father served on that board for eleven years and his teacher before him," and describes taking the seat as fitting into a space long kept for him.
He is troubled by Eunji's children "barely speaking Korean" and by how she has "folded into something else entirely," signaling strong attachment to cultural continuity.
rationales.benevolence: He keeps declining the visit partly because "Someone needs to be here for Mother," prioritizing care obligations to close family over his own travel.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the gallery owner's commercial advice outright, saying "My kiln is not a factory floor" and refusing to make work he would be "embarrassed to put my name on."

rationales.tradition: He dismisses the idea of a Christmas gathering because "We do Seollal and Chuseok. That has always been enough," explicitly favoring inherited observances over adopting a new one.

rationales.security: He takes comfort in stable routine, repeating the year-end firing preparation on the same schedule and concluding that "staying in one place" is "fine."
rationales.tradition: He emphasizes doing the first firing on the "same schedule my teacher kept, same schedule his teacher kept," rooting the work in inherited ritual and continuity.

rationales.conformity: He restrains his impulse to correct the incorrectly tied hanbok because "She would have felt bad" and decides not to create friction during the holiday.
rationales.benevolence: He withholds the criticism out of care for Eunji's feelings and focuses instead on the fact that "The kids looked happy."

rationales.achievement: He spent "the rest of the afternoon comparing my draft" to top-journal papers and fixates on making "visible progress," showing strong investment in excelling by external standards.
rationales.security: He frames the PhD path against what they "gave up" like "a good apartment" and "a pension plan," showing his current choice has actively destabilized safety and predictability.
rationales.benevolence: He hid bad news from Ingrid even though "she's been picking up extra client work" and admits "I should have said something real," which undermines care and openness toward a close partner.

rationales.achievement: "Revised twenty-three pages of the literature review" reflects sustained, concrete effort to improve performance on demanding academic work.
He emphasizes that he has been "working on this for eighteen months" and is distressed by feedback about completion, showing deep investment in proving competence and getting the grant.
rationales.security: He contrasts younger peers with his own "monthly burn rate," wife buying cheaper groceries, and nostalgia for "predictable paychecks," showing the pursuit is straining stability and financial safety.

rationales.benevolence: He stayed after class to walk Willem through the model "step by step" and found meaning in being "just useful," showing concrete care and support for someone he could directly help.

rationales.security: He "ran the numbers" on savings, worries about funding by September, and has started browsing finance jobs "just in case," all of which show active effort to protect stability against threat.

rationales.achievement: He is preoccupied with whether work meets rigorous standards, noting he endured "twenty minutes of pointed questions" and "three weeks revising," which reflects strong concern with competence and evaluation.

rationales.security: After naming "the worst case scenario," he feels lighter because "we're still sitting here, still together," grounding relief in relational stability and safety.
rationales.benevolence: He finally "told her everything," and the exchange centers on mutual care and staying together while "still figuring it out," showing supportive engagement with his partner.

rationales.security: She says she'd "rather be broke" than protect her own savings and keeps lending money she may not get back, which actively undermines her own material stability.
rationales.benevolence: She lends money for Diego's daughters' shoes, drives long distances to help relatives, and says the version of her who stops "feels like a stranger," showing strong devotion to close others' needs.
Even after "twelve hours of driving," she used her break to pick up tia Elena's medication, prioritizing a close family member's needs despite exhaustion.
When the girls offered their "piggy bank money," she "told them to keep it" and was moved to tears, reflecting generosity and emotional investment in close family relationships.

rationales.tradition: "Made tamales with tia Elena for the holidays" and learned "her mother's recipe," directly continuing a family holiday practice handed down across generations.
rationales.benevolence: She chose to spend "the whole afternoon together, just us and the masa," showing warm, attentive time invested in a close relative.

rationales.security: She will "pick up extra shifts" and "maybe skip the new tires for another month" to send more money, which puts her own safety and stability at risk.
rationales.benevolence: She immediately frames helping with rent as "He's my little brother; that's just what you do," showing strong willingness to sacrifice for close family.

rationales.security: Her reaction centers on danger and prevention: "What if next time it's worse?" and she considers having tia Elena move in, showing active concern with protecting someone from future harm.
rationales.benevolence: She feels guilty she "wasn't there" and immediately thinks about changing her living situation to care for tia Elena, reflecting strong responsibility toward a close relative.

rationales.hedonism: She describes New Year's Eve as "nice, actually," enjoys "everyone eat my food and laugh," and imagines ten quiet minutes with coffee, showing a clear turn toward pleasure and restorative enjoyment.
rationales.tradition: The celebration includes "the grape thing at midnight," family food like pernil, and a remembered ritual of her mother's "moment," all tied to inherited family or cultural practices.
rationales.benevolence: She looks at "these people, this noise, this mess" and thinks "this is why I do everything," showing that caring for close others remains her central motivation.

rationales.self_direction: She "said yes before I even finished reading" and admits finishing her coffee would feel like "choosing myself over them," showing difficulty acting on her own chosen need.
rationales.benevolence: She abandons the quiet coffee moment to watch Diego's girls and frames helping them as natural because "the girls are fun to have around," prioritizing close others over herself.

rationales.hedonism: He "got home late again" after staying extra to help and notes Brigitte had already eaten, showing present comfort and enjoyment being sacrificed to duty.
rationales.achievement: "My department scored highest" and he keeps the summary "with the others," showing pride in measurable excellence and external confirmation of his work.
rationales.benevolence: He stayed "an extra forty minutes" to help Katja with the parent complaint, giving time and care to support someone in his immediate work circle.

rationales.achievement: He fixates on the nomination and rechecks "the spreadsheet of student outcomes" to confirm "I'm not slipping," showing strong concern with demonstrated competence and recognition.
rationales.power: The entry centers on status and being publicly overlooked: "nobody thought to put my name forward," which reflects concern with rank and visible standing.

rationales.self_direction: He says "I resent myself for not being able to say not this week," showing he is failing to set his own priorities and direct his time autonomously.
rationales.benevolence: He immediately agrees to review Leon's rubric and help Simone with parents, repeatedly giving his time to people who depend on him.
He forgot to call his sister for her birthday and admits it was "another thing I let slide because the school day swallowed everything," showing neglect of a close relationship.

rationales.hedonism: He "managed the whole afternoon without opening the laptop" and enjoyed a drive and trail walk with Brigitte, clearly making room for rest and enjoyment.
rationales.benevolence: Spending the afternoon reconnecting with Brigitte and reflecting on what matters to her shows attention to a close relationship rather than work demands.

rationales.achievement: Even while trying to dismiss it, his reaction to Markus winning and the mechanical clapping shows continued fixation on competitive recognition and who gets acknowledged.
He spends the evening on Leon's rubric "instead of the conference paper again," recognizing a pattern that undermines his own professional goal.
rationales.benevolence: Reviewing Leon's rubric instead of doing his own work is another concrete choice to support someone in his immediate circle.

rationales.achievement: He proactively "submitted a request to attend the European History Educators Conference," investing in professional growth after four years away.
rationales.benevolence: He "called Hilde properly this time" and listened closely enough that she noticed he sounded "more present," showing care for a close family relationship.

rationales.self_direction: He develops the paper by pulling examples "from my own classroom" and makes focused progress on work that reflects his own thinking rather than others' demands.
rationales.achievement: He writes "two decent pages" and treats closing the laptop before midnight as a "small accomplishment," showing active progress toward a concrete standard of excellence.

rationales.self_direction: He tells the three colleagues he'll "need to scale back" and discovers "Nobody collapsed," showing a clear assertion of his own priorities.
rationales.achievement: After setting that boundary, he returns to the draft and notes "The structure is taking shape," showing deliberate effort toward his own professional work.

He admits that when choosing between relationships and something with "a measurable outcome," he picks the one he can later point to and say "this counted," showing strong prioritization of visible accomplishment.
rationales.benevolence: Brigitte has "stopped inviting the Fischers for dinner" because he cancels or is half-present, and he recognizes his sister noticed the same pattern, showing ongoing neglect of close others.

rationales.achievement: He is willing to return to Nashik again because "the deal isn't done," showing continued effort to secure a business outcome despite exhaustion.
rationales.security: His focus shifts to the fact that "we've got a client audit on Friday," reflecting concern with protecting stability and avoiding operational risk.
rationales.tradition: He notes that "the evening puja helped settle my mind," showing active grounding in an inherited religious practice.

rationales.achievement: He seriously evaluates the biodegradable line, saying "Numbers look solid" and setting a deadline to decide on the capital commitment, which shows goal-oriented business striving.
rationales.tradition: He frames the company as something passed from his father to him and imagines Aarav taking it further, showing attachment to family continuity and inherited roles.

rationales.security: He studies the Q1 numbers, worries the supplier is "squeezing us," and says they "need to find a second source," showing clear attention to protecting the business from threat.
rationales.tradition: Going to Maa's, eating dal "the way Baba used to like it," and feeling steadied by the same table and plates all express strong grounding in family continuity.
rationales.benevolence: He goes to Maa's without being asked and she "just set an extra plate," showing a close, supportive family bond he actively returns to.

rationales.achievement: He compares his company to Rajiv's profile and stresses his own firm's delivery record and scale, showing strong concern with accomplishment and how it measures up.
rationales.power: Lingering over the newspaper profile and wishing for something "more impressive" reflects preoccupation with prestige and public standing.
rationales.benevolence: He admits he wondered whether Aarav would have stayed if the company were bigger, effectively weighing his son's loyalty against business status.

rationales.achievement: He refuses the full week because the biodegradable line decision is a "forty-lakh capital commitment" and "some things you sign yourself," showing work goals taking priority.
rationales.tradition: He values the Varanasi trip for "the aarti" and the whole family gathering, expressing attachment to religious and family rituals.

rationales.achievement: He signs off on the investment and says "It's the right call" and that making the decision "felt good," showing satisfaction in decisive business advancement.
rationales.tradition: He defends his stance by invoking that "My own father worked six days a week until he was seventy-two," explicitly grounding his standard in inherited family norms.

rationales.achievement: He is energized by the trial run and says these are still the moments he likes, "when something you decided on paper becomes real in your hands," showing investment in making work succeed.
rationales.tradition: He notices Aarav kept Maa's brass diya and reflects that "continuing those connections" with Patil's family matters, showing value placed on inherited ties and continuity.

rationales.achievement: He sets a concrete goal to reach spec "by mid-April" and plans customer samples before monsoon, showing active pursuit of a demanding business target.
rationales.tradition: Aarav finding "a small morning puja" and Rajan's response that "something got through" centers the entry on inherited religious continuity.
rationales.benevolence: He moves meetings to come earlier for the family trip and calls Aarav "just wanted to hear his voice," showing deliberate care toward close family.

rationales.hedonism: He says they "ate too much," slept better than he had "in months," and savored the relief of not having to solve problems, showing clear enjoyment and restoration.
rationales.achievement: Even after returning from the trip, he checks the trial results and tells the team to "push for one more round by Friday," maintaining a concrete performance drive.
rationales.tradition: The family gathered for the "aarti" at the ghat and he treasured waking to "temple bells," showing active engagement with valued ritual and place.
rationales.benevolence: He emphasizes being with extended family as "someone's son-in-law, someone's uncle," indicating meaning drawn from close relational roles.

rationales.achievement: He celebrates that the fourth trial "hit target spec" and shares the result proudly, showing strong focus on bringing a difficult project to success.
rationales.tradition: He accepts the board seat partly because "His father and mine started in the same market forty years ago," grounding the decision in continuity with an inherited community legacy.
rationales.universalism: He says joining the board matters because he can "help the next group of small manufacturers," extending concern beyond family and company to a broader community.

rationales.self_direction: She "just nodded along" despite knowing her answer differed, suppressing her own judgment instead of asserting it.
rationales.security: She avoided asking because she wanted "no surprises, no awkward moments," showing a strong preference for predictability and minimizing threat.
rationales.conformity: She says asking a question felt like "making a mess someone else has to clean up," and stayed quiet to avoid disrupting the room.
rationales.tradition: Making "the one my mother used to make" connects the evening to inherited family practice and continuity.

rationales.self_direction: She "wanted to say this is not what we decided" but presented herself as if she "didn't have an opinion at all," deferring her own view.
rationales.security: What upset her was that "we had a plan," and the late restructuring threatened the stability of an agreed approach right before the deadline.
rationales.conformity: She stayed silent because she "didn't want to be the one who drags things backward" and smiled along to preserve group harmony.

rationales.self_direction: She describes "biting the inside of your cheek because you disagree" and still going along, which shows restraint of her own stance.
rationales.conformity: She frames the hard part as not wanting to "make everything tense" and says she has spent a lifetime "reading the room" and letting things go.
rationales.tradition: She contrasts American assertiveness with "I learned different things" and treats those older social habits as meaningful, inherited ways of behaving.

rationales.hedonism: She notices the pleasure of the evening light and "bought a pan dulce from the bakery" to eat on a bench, savoring a small present-moment enjoyment.
rationales.security: She emphasizes working "slowly, one section at a time" because "it helps to have that rhythm," showing comfort in steady structure.
