rationales.universalism: She pushed for "more content on different cultures" because students' families came from places the textbooks "barely mention." Her concern is inclusion and fair representation beyond her own immediate interests.
She is distressed by microplastics in baby formula and by systems that "are supposed to protect people" but do not. The entry centers broad concern for vulnerable children and institutional harm.

rationales.benevolence: She spent time after school helping Amira with reading and was moved by the girl's pride when she finished her first chapter book. The entry centers direct care and encouragement for a specific child in front of her.
rationales.universalism: Volunteering at the community center after school reflects concern that extends beyond formal job duties. She values the moment because it felt "real," tying her effort to broader care for others who need support.

rationales.conformity: She "just nodded and ate" instead of challenging her father, then says she stays quiet to avoid being hurtful and preserve harmony. That is active restraint of her impulse in order to keep the relationship smooth.
rationales.benevolence: In the response she says she was protecting "my dad, mostly" and their relationship by staying quiet. The choice is driven by care for someone close to her, even at personal moral cost.
rationales.universalism: She is concerned about emission rules because "the river matters" and "the kids who swim in it matter." Her attention is on environmental harm and people beyond her immediate circle.

She frames her habits as "paying attention" to issues like ocean acidification and going out of her way to buy organic. The entry shows sustained concern for environmental and societal problems in everyday life.

She spent the afternoon sorting donated winter coats and was moved by the simple logic that "the person in front of you is cold." The entry is about practical care for people in need beyond her immediate personal circle.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I stopped waiting for permission" and started using the last ten minutes her own way with "no approval needed." That is a clear move toward acting on her own judgment inside the institution.
rationales.universalism: The space she created focused on questions like "why some people do not have houses" and "if fish can feel pain." She is helping children engage with fairness and suffering in the wider world.

rationales.conformity: She directly challenged a colleague at lunch and says she does not want to be "the person who stays quiet when it matters." That is active willingness to disrupt social comfort rather than go along.
rationales.universalism: She argues that climate content is not politics but "physical reality" and insists it should not be ignored. The conflict is driven by concern for broader environmental truth and consequences.

rationales.self_direction: He deliberately used "the tracking sheets I designed back in 2019 instead of the new corporate software" and says the company still uses his system because theirs does not work. He is clearly relying on his own judgment over imposed procedure.
rationales.hedonism: He stayed late to sign off reports himself, choosing extra work over rest and saying he was "tired." Comfort and enjoyment are explicitly sacrificed to get the outcome he wants.
rationales.achievement: He emphasizes that "the numbers are right because I checked them" and justifies the extra hours through competence and results. The entry centers performing the work to a high standard.
rationales.conformity: He bypassed the "new corporate software" and ignored the prescribed manual-driven approach because it did not work well. That is active resistance to organizational rules and standard procedure.

rationales.self_direction: He says he listens, then decides which parts "actually apply to us" and keeps only what is useful. That reflects independent judgment rather than passive compliance.
rationales.security: His stated resolution is to "keep doing what works," and he navigates change by preserving the practices that keep "the numbers stay good." The priority is stability and dependable performance over novelty.
rationales.conformity: He describes alignment initiatives as something to sit through and then "quietly go back to what actually functions." That is calculated noncompliance with the expected regional process.
rationales.tradition: He embraces continuity and established methods, saying "old-fashioned works" and defending his whiteboard-based way of solving logistics problems. The entry values tried-and-true practice over newer fashions.

rationales.self_direction: He evaluates the software for himself and says, "If their software improves that number, I will use it. If not, I have the data to explain why we are doing something different." That is explicit insistence on local judgment over top-down directives.
rationales.achievement: He gathered evidence, documenting failures and pulling data showing a "94% on-time delivery rate" so he could make a strong case. The entry is driven by competence, standards, and measurable results.
rationales.conformity: He and Sergio plan to challenge the regional rollout with specifics instead of simply accepting the one-size-fits-all system. He also defends local exceptions against baseline training, rejecting uniform procedure as sufficient.
rationales.benevolence: He took time to explain to the night shift supervisor how to frame the younger workers' confusion and tried to help them understand the reality of the floor. That shows practical support for people around him.

rationales.self_direction: He describes a situation where "my numbers did not matter" and his locally built approach is being replaced by enforced "uniformity." The session is defined by his judgment being overridden.
rationales.power: Despite staying late to prepare with Sergio, he has no sway over the outcome because leadership had already decided. His frustration comes from being denied influence over decisions affecting his department.
rationales.conformity: The decision he is reacting to favors "standardization" and "uniformity" over local evidence. The session shows pressure toward falling in line with a common model rather than allowing exceptions.

rationales.self_direction: Even under the imposed system, he "had to override the automated suggestions manually each time" based on what he knows works locally. He keeps asserting his own judgment instead of surrendering it to the tool.
rationales.power: He is frustrated that "nobody asked us before deciding" and that the plan was simply "handed down." The entry centers his lack of influence over decisions imposed from above.
rationales.conformity: He objects that leadership wants "compliance, not solutions" and repeatedly works around the software rather than just following it. That is clear resistance to imposed norms and procedures.
rationales.benevolence: When "the younger staff kept asking me how to work around the glitches," he helped them navigate the system. The entry shows practical support for the people relying on him day to day.

rationales.self_direction: He contrasts riding deliveries with office life where his "soul leak[s] out through Excel spreadsheets" and says he stayed on the road because he feared going back to performance reviews and promotions.
rationales.stimulation: He says delivery work means "motion, the doing" and that on the road he is "running toward something," showing attraction to movement and a less predictable life.
rationales.achievement: He says he was scared to "slip back into caring about quarterly targets and performance reviews" and to measure his worth in promotions, rejecting conventional success metrics.

rationales.self_direction: He refuses to quietly accept his brother-in-law's judgment and insists that "not everyone needs a business card to feel important," asserting his own definition of worth.
rationales.power: He dismisses status markers by mocking the need for a "business card to feel important" and belittling insurance work built on making people "afraid of the future."
rationales.conformity: He admits he "could have just let it go" but instead made family dinner awkward and "embarrassed her in front of her family," showing little restraint for the sake of harmony.

rationales.tradition: He notices the old Geylang shophouses disappearing and feels that "someone should notice what we're losing," expressing concern for preserving inherited places and continuity with the past.
rationales.benevolence: He spends time "helping sort donations" and teaching Ramesh English on Saturdays, taking concrete supportive action for people he knows personally.
rationales.universalism: He reflects on migrant workers who "send half their salary home" and feels compelled to witness urban loss and social inequality, showing concern beyond his immediate circle.

rationales.hedonism: He treasures the simple pleasure of dinner where "her laugh came easy. Mine too" and says these moments, not money, are what he is working for.
rationales.achievement: He notes that a "twelve-hour shift" let him "hit my weekly target," showing effort toward a concrete performance goal.
rationales.benevolence: He frames his work around being present with Priya and values sitting across from her "without apology," centering care for a close relationship.

rationales.security: He says he missed "the end-of-year bonuses" and even "the illusion of security," and he is seriously considering a more stable agency job opening.

rationales.self_direction: He resists a return to office hierarchy and says he wants "a different kind of plan" that does not feel like surrender, trying to author life on his own terms.
rationales.stimulation: He questions whether he wants "the uncertainty, the lack of a plan" for another year, treating unpredictability as something he may no longer want.
rationales.security: His recurring concern is the absence of stability: he worries that having no plan is "its own kind of trap" and starts thinking about a more secure direction.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects outside definitions of success, insisting that "some of us just refuse to let corporations define what success looks like."
rationales.power: He pushes back on status-based judgment in the claim that gig workers are "lacking ambition," rejecting prestige hierarchies as the measure of a worthwhile life.

rationales.stimulation: He wonders if he has been "confusing restlessness with meaning" and admires the courage of ordinary repetition, moving away from novelty-seeking as an ideal.
rationales.security: Priya's fears about injury, regulation changes, and retirement land with him, and he admits she is "not wrong" about the risks of his unstable life.
rationales.benevolence: Instead of arguing, he takes Priya's fear seriously and reflects on what kind of life supports enduring family scenes and "showing up, day after day."

rationales.conformity: He stayed quiet because it was "a party" and he did not want to "make it weird," prioritizing smooth social interaction over speaking up.
rationales.universalism: He personally knows the asylum-seeker families being discussed, yet "just sat there" while they were described as a neighborhood problem.

He is troubled by the sandwich packaging because he studies environmental harm, buys less meat, cycles, and resists the idea that saying "it doesn't matter" about one thing should become indifference to everything.

rationales.conformity: He "pretended I was busy with the coffee machine" instead of intervening, choosing social ease over confrontation.
rationales.universalism: He overheard a joke about a Somali student's accent and did nothing, failing to defend someone outside his immediate circle from prejudicial treatment.

rationales.hedonism: He ordered from Foodora twice because he "couldn't face making pasta," choosing immediate comfort and relief from effort.
rationales.universalism: He knowingly used chains that "treat their delivery riders badly" and admits "my tiredness won," prioritizing convenience over concern for exploited workers.

rationales.achievement: He is tempted because the liaison role would be "good for my CV" and he admits liking that his supervisor "smiled when she said my name" and chose him.
rationales.universalism: He seriously considers a role tied to a company whose record on lignite mining and "indigenous Sámi land rights" he already knows to be harmful.

rationales.achievement: He treats the liaison role's appeal as "convenient for my career" and something that would pad LinkedIn, but describes that temptation as an ethical compromise rather than a goal worth pursuing.
rationales.universalism: He centers people harmed beyond his own circle, thinking about "Fatima and her daughter" and "the Sámi reindeer herders," and sees taking the role as betraying those concerns.

rationales.self_direction: He independently tells Lindqvist he will "pass on the Vattenfall liaison position," rejecting a path that would have helped his CV.
rationales.conformity: With Anders, he substitutes a mild point about waiting lists for direct correction and says he keeps being "diplomatic in the moment," prioritizing social smoothness.

rationales.security: He says he chose "the comfortable academic route" because it is "safer," favoring protected distance over more exposed direct work.
rationales.universalism: He frames his thesis work as staying in a "well-heated Swedish apartment" while others are already losing homes, and contrasts himself with a friend building flood shelters who is "actually doing something."

rationales.hedonism: He admits he declined the complaint letter because he "just didn't want another thing to carry," avoiding extra strain in the moment.
rationales.universalism: He lied that he was too busy with his thesis when asked to help draft a complaint about poor housing conditions, refusing a concrete chance to support vulnerable people.

rationales.security: He chooses the cheaper coffee because he was "over budget for the month" and thought, "I can't afford to care about this right now," prioritizing financial caution.
rationales.universalism: He abandons his usual Fairtrade purchase for the store brand and is disturbed by how "convenience becomes the default," showing a concrete lapse in ethical concern for broader others.

rationales.achievement: He persisted through "three days of reading documentation" until "those tests finally went green," showing sustained effort to solve a difficult technical problem.

rationales.conformity: He rejects the expected relational script, saying "Sometimes I do not want to perform that script," which signals resistance to maintaining smooth social expectations.
rationales.benevolence: He let Fatima's call ring and lied that he was "in a meeting," withdrawing from a close relationship instead of engaging with her needs.

rationales.achievement: He spent "two hours explaining borrow checker errors" and was told he should teach the whole team, reflecting visible competence and recognition.
rationales.benevolence: He invested significant time helping Emma understand Rust, "watching her face shift from confusion to clarity," which is direct support for a colleague.

rationales.security: He has "already packed" and "already checked in online" and keeps refreshing the weather app, showing a strong focus on preparedness and reducing uncertainty before travel.

rationales.self_direction: "I left at 56" frames his move as a personally chosen break from expected life patterns, prioritizing his own path over family expectations.
rationales.benevolence: He says his mother keeps asking about him and admits "I probably will not" call her, avoiding care and responsiveness toward a close family member.

rationales.achievement: He led a session for "fifteen people, including the CTO," then handled questions afterward, showing competence that was publicly recognized.
rationales.benevolence: He shared knowledge with the team by walking them through concepts and answering questions, directly helping others learn.

rationales.self_direction: He feels pushed away from coding toward documentation and only "nodded and said I would think about it," suggesting external direction overriding what he wants to do.

rationales.hedonism: Watching someone sing badly without caring makes him realize, "I have not felt that kind of careless in a long time," highlighting distance from spontaneous enjoyment and pleasure.

rationales.self_direction: He frames the past six months as "proving I could start over," emphasizing a self-authored life choice rather than staying with what was expected.
rationales.stimulation: He chose upheaval and uncertainty, reflecting on "learning syntax" and not knowing "what comes next," which shows engagement with novelty and challenge.
rationales.security: He notes the lease ending, the project may or may not ship, and that he has "more continuity than anything else in my life right now" from a kebab order, revealing active instability rather than settled safety.

rationales.self_direction: He says staying in Amman felt like repeating "the same episode" and that being in Berlin is better than being where "everyone expected me to be," showing a deliberate choice of his own path.
rationales.stimulation: He moved countries "on a whim" because he "needed to not know what was going to happen next," explicitly embracing uncertainty and challenge.
rationales.security: He says he was "terrified" and still is most days, but prefers that fear to the certainty he left behind, indicating a willingness to forgo stability.

rationales.benevolence: She noticed the new resident was overwhelmed and "walked her through" the paperwork, giving direct, practical support to someone nearby.

rationales.power: Her expertise was overridden when Katja cut in and the room moved on "as though I hadn't been speaking," leaving her silent and without influence in the discussion.

rationales.security: She describes a stable weekend of shopping, meal prep, cleaning, and planning Christmas sleeping arrangements as "manageable," showing preference for order and practical predictability.

rationales.achievement: The patient's daughter "named the exact thing I did" and made her feel seen, which is clear recognition of competent work.
rationales.benevolence: She carefully explained a patient's medication changes and the daughter said it mattered, showing concrete care for others' wellbeing.

rationales.power: She says the hardest part is "not deciding" and wishes she could approve the staffing request herself, expressing a strong drive for authority and control over the situation.
Although she had pushed the renovation through and wanted to defend it, she "gave in" and stopped arguing, relinquishing influence in the moment.
rationales.conformity: She conceded because "fighting about it at Christmas dinner felt worse than conceding," restraining herself to preserve social harmony.

rationales.power: She felt affirmed when her son wanted her opinion and it felt like being "consulted, properly consulted," which centers respect and interpersonal influence.
rationales.tradition: She values that he made "his father's old Kartoffelsalat recipe," highlighting continuity with family custom and the past.
rationales.benevolence: She spent an hour helping her son compare insurance policies, offering attentive support and care within a close relationship.

rationales.power: The staffing request was finally approved and she spent the morning "updating rosters," putting her back in an effective coordinating role over ward operations.

rationales.conformity: He "didn't say a word" because "nobody wants that fight at the table," showing restraint to preserve social harmony during Christmas dinner.

rationales.security: He chose the cheaper supplier because it would "actually turn a real profit for once" and help "keep the lights on," prioritizing business stability.
rationales.universalism: He knowingly ordered from a factory "flagged... for dumping chemical waste into a river that feeds into local farmland," accepting likely environmental harm for broader communities.

rationales.security: He refuses to pause the donation program even though it is the "biggest cost center" and pausing it would put the company "in the black," choosing against financial stability.
rationales.universalism: After reading that a family's daughter "stopped getting rashes" from the filter, he decides "I'm not pausing the donations," prioritizing help for people beyond his immediate circle.
He visits the reservation, checks that the clean-water units are working, and feels renewed by seeing "the water is clean" and helping a family use it to grow food.
He coordinates shipments for donation units and pushes back on using people's "water problems as marketing copy," landing on an opt-in approach that respects the communities being helped.

rationales.security: He switches to a domestic supplier that "costs more" and accepts that "margins will be tight for a while," sacrificing financial stability.
rationales.universalism: He cancels the Guangzhou order because he "couldn't stop thinking about it," reversing a choice tied to suspected environmental damage.

rationales.achievement: "Three years of work" culminated in the municipal committee approving her exhibition proposal, marking clear success in a long creative effort.
rationales.security: The grant means she can stop "checking my account every morning" and her mother is relieved she won't have to worry about rent for a while.
rationales.universalism: The installation centers on "rising waters" and "families in Bangladesh," showing direct concern for climate displacement and broader human suffering.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects the idea that the "responsible thing" is to quit art for a "real job" and instead keeps choosing her own path despite pressure.
rationales.security: She continues with uncertain grant-based work, saying "I'm too old for this uncertainty" yet still not choosing the safer path.
rationales.universalism: She is consumed by research on "millions displaced" and wants to make something "true" that responds to large-scale harm beyond her own life.

She is devastated by "another boat capsized in the Mediterranean" and recalls making work from refugee stories so people like Fatima could be heard, showing deep concern for suffering beyond her immediate circle.

rationales.benevolence: She carefully buys her mother "a scarf, some chocolate, a book," focusing on thoughtful care for a close family member.
She spends a quiet Christmas watching her mother sleep and thinking about "how many more of these we might have," showing tenderness toward a close loved one.
rationales.universalism: She says she has "always cared too much" and wants to "keep paying attention to what hurts," explicitly tying her life to the world's pain and displacement.

rationales.self_direction: Although she smiles and nods in the meeting, she decides "The work will be what it is" and would rather they "find someone else" than reshape it to fit expectations.
rationales.security: She resists the funder's request even though "she controls my funding," putting financial stability at risk.
rationales.universalism: Her refusal to make the piece more palatable is grounded in solidarity with people "drowning" and a commitment to represent that suffering truthfully.
She reframes hope as "a form of resistance" and adds handwritten messages from people who "rebuilt their lives after displacement," centering the voices of affected communities.

rationales.hedonism: He plans "four extra hours a day" despite his back already hurting, choosing strain and self-denial over comfort.
rationales.security: He "ran the numbers" and takes on extra late-night work to make sure Priya's fee increase can be covered, prioritizing financial stability.
rationales.benevolence: He cannot imagine telling Amma to find another way for Priya's fees, so he commits to extra work to support his family.

rationales.achievement: The renovation opportunity feels like "the first real opening in months," and he wants to prove himself at the Saturday site visit.
rationales.security: He values that the work is "steady weekend work" and could let him drop late-night delivery while still covering expenses.
rationales.benevolence: He frames the opportunity in terms of being able to "still cover Priya's fees without grinding myself into the ground," linking the decision to family support.
After hitting his target early, he uses the extra time to buy curry puffs for the chess-playing uncles downstairs, a small act of care for familiar people around him.

rationales.hedonism: He stays up worrying and loses sleep after a "forty minute" call even though he has "an early morning slot booked," sacrificing rest.
rationales.benevolence: He remains on the phone late into the night "just letting her talk" when Priya is distressed, prioritizing her emotional support.

rationales.achievement: Mr. Tan says "you clearly know what you're doing" and offers him two days a week, a clear sign of competence being recognized.
rationales.security: He immediately calculates that the higher hourly rate could let him "actually rest on Sundays for once," showing the appeal of a more stable and sustainable income mix.

rationales.hedonism: He covers extra late-night shifts until "my knee is swollen," showing clear self-denial and physical strain.
rationales.security: He keeps Priya's money and books "the polyclinic appointment for Monday," taking a concrete step toward protecting his health and stability.
rationales.benevolence: He covers shifts because "Suresh helped me when I first started" and keeps Priya's gift because rejecting it would hurt her, showing sustained care for close others.

rationales.self_direction: She says "my hand knows what it wants to make but the brief says otherwise" and then accepts that she must "read the room" instead of following her own artistic judgment.
rationales.conformity: She expects to "adjust" because "that's how these things work," showing restraint to fit the committee's expectations and keep harmony.
rationales.universalism: Her real sketch keeps returning to "river contamination," showing sustained concern with environmental harm rather than a purely decorative brief.

rationales.self_direction: She contrasts David's freedom with her own habit of "hold[ing] back," indicating she is not acting with the same independent honesty she admires.
rationales.security: She explains her restraint through practical pressures like "a landlord" and worries about whether "the grants are coming through," showing stability concerns shaping her choices.
rationales.universalism: She is moved by David's collage about neighborhood flooding with "garbage and river water mixed together," which centers broader environmental harm and community suffering.

rationales.self_direction: She says yes to a project with "no committee, no brief, no polite rewrites," embracing a more autonomous and self-authored way of working.
rationales.universalism: She joins a march about "deforestation in Guaviare," studies satellite images of the cleared forest, and sketches families affected by it, showing direct concern for environmental destruction and displaced people.

rationales.self_direction: She "compromised" and made the contamination imagery more abstract, then describes years of "things I chose not to say" and work whose "edges got filed down."
rationales.conformity: She accepts that "public art is negotiation" and follows what the committee and family caution seem to require rather than insisting on the direct version she wanted.
rationales.universalism: Even in compromise, the work remains focused on river contamination and the march, showing continued attention to environmental damage and its public meaning.

rationales.self_direction: She drafts the response she really wants, then deletes it and settles for "a sad emoji," describing the result as "utterly hollow."
rationales.conformity: She silences herself because she can already picture "the awkward silence at Easter dinner," prioritizing family smoothness over direct disagreement.
rationales.universalism: Her unsent response focuses on "logging companies," lack of enforcement, and families "being displaced," showing clear concern for justice beyond her immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: She says the mural is "honest work" and that "the paint is saying exactly what I mean," indicating direct expression without softening herself for others.
rationales.universalism: This is the march mural tied to the deforestation protest, so the honesty she celebrates is in service of a broader environmental cause.

rationales.self_direction: She tells her mother "it's just a painting, nothing political," even though she admits that is "not entirely true," hiding her real stance rather than expressing it plainly.
rationales.conformity: She downplays the mural's meaning to avoid upsetting her mother, adapting her message to preserve smooth family relations.
rationales.benevolence: She explicitly says "she doesn't need to carry that" and wants to protect her mother from worry, centering a close other's emotional burden.

rationales.self_direction: Despite wanting to hide, she has already said yes to speaking and thinks "this matters more than anyone's comfort, including mine," showing movement toward acting on conviction.
rationales.security: She keeps focusing on exposure, her "name attached to words," and family fallout, yet is preparing to step into that uncertainty anyway.
rationales.universalism: Her mural depicts the forest "dissolving into ash" with displaced families, and the speech is tied to making that environmental harm publicly visible.

rationales.self_direction: She actually speaks at the march even though her "voice shook," and afterward feels "glad I did it once," indicating she acted on her own convictions rather than staying hidden.
rationales.security: She had imagined a family "catastrophe" but spoke anyway, taking a personally risky and exposing step despite fearing the consequences.
rationales.universalism: Her speech was about people "losing their home" and making environmental loss tangible, reflecting concern for harmed communities beyond her immediate circle.

rationales.achievement: She spent three months working on the protocol on "my own time" because she wanted to "prove that I could contribute beyond bedside care," showing strong investment in competence and recognition.
She takes extra shifts because "being reliable matters when you're trying to move up," directly tying her effort to advancement and performance.
rationales.benevolence: She talks a new grad through "her first code situation" step by step while doing compressions, giving practical support to someone in immediate need.

rationales.achievement: She keeps refreshing the posting for the "junior ward manager" role, showing active preoccupation with advancement and external progress markers.
She keeps checking work emails at home because "falling behind means being invisible" and says "I can't just be average," showing strong drive for recognition and standing out.
rationales.benevolence: Even at home she prioritizes work monitoring over connection with Thomas, despite acknowledging "he wasn't wrong" about never really having her attention.

rationales.hedonism: She describes extreme overwork, saying she couldn't remember "the last time I ate an actual meal during a shift" or left on time, which shows present comfort and enjoyment being sacrificed.
rationales.achievement: She keeps enduring the strain because of the coming review and says what would make it worth it is "the title" and "the recognition," clearly centering success and validation.
rationales.security: She is exhausted, skipping breaks and meals, and no longer feels physically steady, which actively undermines personal stability and protection.

rationales.achievement: She accepts the training duty because "it's exposure" and "a chance to show the department head what I can do," treating it as a route to advancement.
rationales.conformity: Her "what else am I going to say?" and "you don't get anywhere by saying no" show willing compliance with workplace expectations even when she resents the arrangement.

rationales.achievement: She hesitates to commit to Thomas's cousin's wedding because work scheduling still takes priority over making the relationship plan definite.
rationales.benevolence: Even though she knows she could "probably make it work," she withholds commitment to an important family event, neglecting a close partner's need for dependable support.

rationales.achievement: She takes the praise that she made difficult protocols "actually make sense" as a "small win," showing satisfaction in demonstrated competence.
rationales.benevolence: The entry centers helping new hires understand critical procedures better, which is concrete support for close coworkers in her immediate environment.

rationales.achievement: She missed the anniversary because she dismissed the reminder "during a shift," showing work obligations overriding personal milestones.
rationales.benevolence: Forgetting the anniversary and only noticing after Thomas quietly "made dinner like normal" shows neglect of a close relationship's emotional importance.

rationales.achievement: She spends the evening building a self-assessment around "all the overtime, the certifications, the protocol work" so it will be "enough," which squarely reflects striving for recognized competence.

rationales.hedonism: She lingers over glazing and says she "forgot about everything else" watching the color catch, then enjoys stopping for knafeh with her mother. These moments center sensory pleasure and present-moment enjoyment.
rationales.benevolence: She immediately stops work when "Mama called" and drives her to the pharmacy, then stays present through the outing. The choice clearly prioritizes a close family member's needs over her own plans.

rationales.self_direction: She says yes to the painting because "what else do you say" with family watching, even though she feels overextended. Her own needs are explicitly subordinated to external pressure.
rationales.hedonism: The "glass of wine on the balcony" is described as "the best part of my whole week," and she longs for a Dead Sea day trip to "float," "eat too much," and laugh. She is clearly craving restorative pleasure rather than more duty.
rationales.conformity: She agrees to unpaid work because family expectation makes refusal feel socially impossible, and worries wanting time for herself "sounds selfish." The decision is driven by preserving harmony and meeting expectations.
rationales.benevolence: Despite resentment, she takes on the painting because Nadia is "going through a divorce" and keeps covering pharmacy runs for her mother. The entry shows sustained support for close others under strain.

She runs the workshop attentively enough to notice Sana carrying her turtle pot "with both hands like it was made of glass." The entry centers caring, encouraging involvement with the children around her.

rationales.self_direction: After describing nonstop family demands, she starts "a new sketch" that "No one asked for" and insists "it's mine." Making something purely on her own terms is a clear act of personal authorship.
rationales.benevolence: She takes over the pharmacy run again because otherwise "Mama has to take a taxi alone and feel like a burden." The choice is still anchored in protecting and caring for a close family member.

rationales.self_direction: She reclaims her art as "something that's mine" and affirms that "the studio, the clay, the color mixing" belong to her even amid obligations. The entry strongly emphasizes an autonomous creative life.
rationales.hedonism: She treats herself to "good coffee" with cardamom, drinks it slowly in the garden, and says the coffee, Dead Sea trips, and balcony wine are how she keeps from hollowing out. Pleasure is framed as an important need, not a luxury.
rationales.benevolence: She finishes Nadia's painting, brings it over, stays to make tea, and offers emotional support while Nadia opens up about her marriage. The session is full of concrete care for a family member in distress.

After noticing Meera looked overwhelmed, she walks with her and shows her how to break the transition into smaller steps, then keeps it quiet to avoid embarrassing her. This is careful, interpersonal support for someone in her immediate circle.
She says there was "no question" about staying with Amma and taking her to the clinic. The decision plainly prioritizes care for a close family member.
rationales.universalism: While sitting in the clinic, she keeps thinking about the tutoring-center children waiting for the geometry worksheets she promised. Her concern extends beyond family to the welfare of children outside her immediate circle.

rationales.security: She immediately worries about "my pension" and whether "the timing would be right," describing retirement as full of "uncertainties." The entry is focused on stability and future assurance.
rationales.conformity: She tells her brother "I would see what is possible" because she "did not want to disappoint him" even though she prefers to keep working. Social expectation shapes her response more than her own preference.

rationales.achievement: She tracks a concrete improvement outcome: "Ravi, who was failing in September, scored 72%." The entry highlights successful performance against a measurable standard.

rationales.self_direction: She believes strongly that "talent is not distributed by birth" but only offers a mild reply and changes the subject. She acts against her own convictions rather than voicing them freely.
