rationales.security: He realizes 'I might have already arrived-and this is it,' a destabilizing recognition that the future he counted on may not materialize as expected.
rationales.benevolence: Lucia 'made me coffee and sat with me,' and his ability to register that care keeps the entry anchored in close relational support rather than pure status resentment.

rationales.self_direction: He deliberately rethinks how he 'keep[s] score,' choosing to consider a different measure of a successful career than titles or recognition from above.
rationales.benevolence: He finds meaning in 'the people who learned something from me and kept climbing' and in plants running well because 'someone I trained is running them,' centering care and support for close professional relationships.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects pressure from colleagues, saying "I'm not going to apologize for it" after being criticized for his "field trip habit."
rationales.conformity: He knowingly goes against school norms by choosing garden visits over test prep despite a break-room jab from another teacher.
rationales.benevolence: He centers a student's growth, describing how watching Marcus Jr. engage "for the first time all year" is what mattered most.
rationales.universalism: He wants students to understand "they're part of something bigger than standardized tests" and connect with food systems and the natural world.

rationales.tradition: He avoids participating in the family's "60th anniversary dinner" and admits he never practices the Cantonese speech expected of him, pulling away from an inherited language and ritual.
rationales.benevolence: He "let it go to voicemail" instead of calling his grandmother back even though he knows the conversation matters to her.

rationales.self_direction: He decides to go beyond district materials anyway, saying "I'm also going to bring in my family photos and tell the real story" despite the principal's warning.
rationales.conformity: He pushes against "don't go off curriculum" expectations by planning to teach material the principal signaled she did not want.
rationales.tradition: He insists on sharing "my family photos" and his great-grandfather's story, treating inherited family history as something that should be preserved and passed on.
rationales.universalism: He objects to the curriculum minimizing the Chinese Exclusion Act and wants students to hear "the real story," showing concern for historical injustice and marginalized people.

rationales.benevolence: He frames teaching around what he wants his students to carry with them, saying the job is about moments where "kids discover something true about the world."
rationales.universalism: The whole exchange centers on interconnection: "we must be connected to each other too and to the fish and to the trees," and he explicitly wants them to learn that "we're all connected."

rationales.hedonism: He buys the strawberries because he was "tired" and "wanted something sweet," prioritizing immediate comfort and pleasure.
rationales.universalism: He knowingly makes a choice he sees as environmentally harmful, dwelling on strawberries "from Chile" wrapped in plastic and admitting his action "don't align with" what he teaches about sustainability.

rationales.tradition: He agrees to give the anniversary speech "in Cantonese" and ties it to the family's restaurant history and community meaning.
rationales.benevolence: He makes the choice because "It's important to her" and commits to practicing with his grandmother to support something that matters to his family.
He says he pushed for the assembly because he remembers feeling alone and does not want "any of my students to feel that way."
rationales.universalism: He brings in speakers from multiple cultural and religious communities and celebrates students learning that "the world is bigger than their own street."

rationales.tradition: The entry is built around Lunar New Year preparation, remembered dumpling-folding, and family stories repeated across generations.
rationales.benevolence: He spends the day cooking with his mother and reflects with gratitude that "she's still here to correct me," showing warmth and closeness toward family.
He says helping even "one kid skip that confusion" and feel whole is "why I became a teacher," centering care for the students directly in front of him.

rationales.tradition: He keeps practicing a speech about what the restaurant has meant over sixty years and calls it "history" and "proof we belong here."
rationales.benevolence: He works with his grandmother on the speech and tries to honor what the restaurant means "not just to our family but to the town," showing commitment to people tied to his life.

rationales.self_direction: He chooses for himself what matters most, leaving the meeting and saying "I'm not going to let a faculty meeting come between me and honoring that."
rationales.conformity: He breaks workplace expectations by leaving a faculty meeting early and accepts that the principal may note it.
rationales.tradition: He treats the anniversary dinner and family restaurant as something sacred to honor, calling it "proof that we made it here" and "built something."
rationales.benevolence: He leaves because "my grandmother is counting on me" and "the whole family is counting on me," prioritizing their needs over routine work.

rationales.self_direction: He refuses to let others define his path, insisting "I'm not going to apologize" for pursuing growth instead of "coasting."
rationales.stimulation: He is drawn to the expedition because it is "exactly the kind of opportunity that doesn't come twice," showing appetite for challenge and novelty.
rationales.achievement: He frames the decision through expansion and performance, pointing to building the company from "nothing" and wanting "to grow."
rationales.security: He presses ahead even though "the company is already stretched thin," favoring opportunity over stability.
rationales.universalism: He emphasizes that the project is for "sustainable tourism" and that they were trusted "to do it right," signaling concern for broader responsible impact.

rationales.stimulation: He highlights the thrill of novelty in "standing somewhere new, seeing what could be."
rationales.achievement: He is already envisioning execution and results, "picturing the first group coming through in February" after a successful scouting day.

rationales.tradition: He recreates a family routine because "that's what Abuela always made in October," and treasures using "the same" cracked wooden spoon.
rationales.benevolence: He gives his Sunday to a big multigenerational family gathering and treats the crowded table as something that "matters more than whatever else I could be doing."

rationales.self_direction: He explicitly contrasts his chosen path with his father's, saying he used those business tools for "something he never would have chosen."
rationales.achievement: He describes the proposal as "clean, professional, ambitious" and intentionally asks for more funding to execute the vision at a high level.
rationales.security: He asks for "twice the funding we technically need because I want a buffer," prioritizing protection against future risk.
rationales.tradition: He reflects on inheritance directly, saying "Maybe that's what inheritance actually looks like" as he carries forward his father's teachings in a new form.
rationales.universalism: He wants to bring in "local guides from Chocó communities from day one, not as an afterthought," showing respect for inclusion and fairer community participation.

rationales.stimulation: In the response he says that when he's "navigating a new river or negotiating with a community for the first time" he feels most like himself.
rationales.security: He calls family ritual "the anchor" and says without something to return to "you're just moving for the sake of moving," showing a need for stable grounding.
rationales.tradition: The entry revolves around repeated Sunday customs, his mother's familiar criticism, and "the same story about my uncle Jorge and the goat from 1987."
rationales.benevolence: He personally drives to pick up his unwell mother and embraces the caring family rhythm instead of treating it as a burden.

rationales.stimulation: After the approval he spends hours staring at maps "like a kid planning a treasure hunt," showing excitement for exploration and adventure.
rationales.achievement: The emotional reaction follows a concrete success: the regional government approved the proposal with "full funding."

rationales.security: He is shaken because a key employee quit "a month before our biggest project," directly threatening stability and predictability.

rationales.stimulation: He admires María's "trial by fire" attitude and says of his cousin's steady restaurant life, "I just don't think it's my kind of honor," preferring movement over repetition.
rationales.tradition: He marks Day of the Dead with the family ofrenda, using "photos, marigolds, Papá's favorite rum" and telling Tomás "we do it anyway, because the doing is what matters."
rationales.benevolence: He invests in family ties through dinner with Esperanza and by including Tomás in the ofrenda ritual, treating these close relationships as meaningful obligations.

rationales.stimulation: He says "The gap between dreaming and doing is where I live. Sometimes it feels like flying," embracing uncertainty and challenge.
rationales.achievement: He is focused on whether the team can actually "build" the ambitious project he has envisioned on the ground.
rationales.universalism: His success criteria center on community dignity and impact beyond the company, wanting "community members who see themselves in the project" and guests who leave changed.

rationales.self_direction: He reframes the trip as authentic to himself, saying "it's not recklessness, it's how I'm built" and admitting he is done being defensive about it.
rationales.stimulation: He explicitly names "the adventure part" as something he needs, not just the work itself.
rationales.hedonism: His mother's words "también diviértete" matter because she finally recognizes his desire for enjoyment and fun as legitimate.

rationales.self_direction: He says the guilt lifted when he stopped expecting others to understand and accepted "this is who I am," choosing self-authorship over outside approval.
rationales.stimulation: Despite discomforts, he says "my mind is alive" after days of route-mapping, travel, and unpredictable field conditions.
rationales.tradition: He concludes that he can "honor where you came from and still keep moving," explicitly integrating movement with loyalty to roots.
rationales.universalism: He insists "We can't bring tourists in like we own the place" and that they must "earn the right to be there," showing respect for local communities and place.

rationales.stimulation: He savors "the routine of it" and says the route works because "nothing is asking me to figure anything out," showing comfort with sameness rather than novelty.
rationales.hedonism: He says the walk, snow, and "the soup I made this morning" made him "just felt good," treating sensory comfort as a meaningful good in itself.
rationales.security: He is soothed by "same walk, same coat, same apartment" and by a route that is "automatic," showing clear attachment to predictability and stability.
The possibility that the landlord "might sell the building" leaves him sitting on his bed "for twenty minutes staring at the wall," showing how strongly housing instability unsettles him.

rationales.stimulation: He rejects uncertainty outright, describing the apartment as something he chose because it "felt more predictable" and reacting angrily to "just deal with it when it happens."
rationales.security: The whole entry is organized around threat to stable housing and finances: he keeps "running the numbers," maps scenarios, and mourns that the thing he picked for being "steady" may collapse.
He frames the higher premium as "another thing eroding the margin I built into my budget," showing active concern with preserving financial cushion and predictability.
He weighs the conference against shaky housing and says spending "$800-1,200 on a maybe-partially-funded trip" feels reckless, explicitly prioritizing financial stability over opportunity.
He describes the developer talks as "the clock is ticking on something I can't control" and calls "Months" not a timeline but "a fog," showing deep distress at prolonged instability.
rationales.benevolence: He hides the housing problem from his mother because "She worries and then she offers money she doesn't really have," protecting a close family member from emotional and financial strain.

rationales.achievement: Submitting the chapter "Two months ahead of the internal deadline" after blocking out time every morning shows disciplined effort toward a clear performance standard.
rationales.security: He says he "can't function when something is hanging over me unfinished" and values that "the plan actually held," showing strong relief in order and predictability.

He chooses the roommate option because Priya is "consistent," the rent can be budgeted, and knowing where he'll live means he can "actually sleep," all of which center stability and reduced uncertainty.

rationales.hedonism: She lingers over sensory pleasure, noting the kitchen "smelled good for hours" and that eating cake with hot Milo alone in the quiet morning "was nice."
She gives up the CNY market with friends that they had been planning "for two weeks," setting aside a pleasurable outing without hesitation.
rationales.conformity: Her response, "what am I going to say — no ... She's my daughter," shows she is guided by family-role expectations and what feels socially proper.
rationales.benevolence: She immediately agrees to watch the grandkids because her daughter needs help, prioritizing close family obligations over her own plans.

rationales.hedonism: Even when exhausted, she "couldn't even let myself have" ten minutes on the sofa and abandons the long bath she wanted, showing active denial of rest and comfort.
rationales.benevolence: Her attention stays fixed on the grandchildren's needs, feeling she should be "cutting fruit, tidying up, checking if they needed water" rather than resting herself.

rationales.hedonism: She knows "a massage would help" and that lying down with a heat pad would help, but denies herself that relief and keeps going despite pain.
rationales.tradition: Her decision is shaped by "my mother's voice" saying not to be "so pampered" and by the older-generation comparison about carrying "buckets of water uphill," showing deference to inherited norms.

rationales.hedonism: She deliberately stays in the queue for laksa, then enjoys it fully by sitting alone, not checking her phone, and deciding the melting ice cream was "worth it."
She wants to sit with tea and enjoy the pastry, but chores and calls take over, and she ends up eating it "over the sink" while already thinking "three tasks ahead."
rationales.benevolence: She automatically saves "the rest of the box for the grandkids," explaining that "Of course you give the nice things to the children," which centers close family over herself.
She spends her Sunday visit bringing her mother what she asked for, adjusting the aircon, getting an extra blanket, and refilling her thermos, all focused on caring for a close family member.

rationales.hedonism: Although "$38 for an hour" is affordable, she feels she must "justify it" and "have done enough to deserve an hour of someone rubbing my feet," showing reluctance to allow herself simple pleasure.
She follows through on the reflexology, says "It was good," and notes it "actually made a difference," showing a concrete choice in favor of comfort and enjoyment despite later guilt.
rationales.benevolence: The outing includes sustained warm connection with Mei, and she highlights that talking about the wedding planning felt "normal, like two friends just doing something together," reflecting investment in a close relationship.

rationales.hedonism: She spent Sunday lesson planning and taking on "four families now with custom homework requests," leaving little room for rest or enjoyment.
rationales.conformity: She "said yes before I even finished reading" another parent request, showing automatic compliance with others' expectations.
rationales.benevolence: She agrees to extra worksheets for families and tells her mother "she doesn't need to worry" so her mother can focus on Dad's surgery.
She notices Aiden's vulnerability after his parents' split and responds gently, saying she wants to build a room he can "trust... enough to risk it."

rationales.self_direction: She says she is "scared she'll go to Principal Davis" and hates that she avoids standing up for what she actually thinks is right.
rationales.hedonism: She is "drafting a custom enrichment packet at 9pm" for a family demand she does not believe in, sacrificing comfort for obligation.
rationales.security: She explicitly chooses what feels "easier" because pushing back would "make the rest of the year harder," prioritizing stability and risk avoidance.
rationales.conformity: She "nodded through the whole thing" and promised extra work to keep the parent satisfied rather than challenge the demand.
rationales.benevolence: She admits the child "is actually right at grade level" while "eight kids reading below benchmark" need more attention, yet she redirects effort away from those greater needs.

rationales.self_direction: She disagrees with losing instruction days but "didn't say anything either," suppressing her own judgment.
rationales.conformity: When "everyone nodded along," she went along with the mandate instead of objecting.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I don't know how to say no" and realizes "probably nothing terrible" would have happened if she had refused, showing weak authorship over her own commitments.
rationales.hedonism: She is already overloaded with book club and bus duty, yet adds another obligation that drains her time and energy.
rationales.conformity: She agreed because "everyone turned and smiled" and because refusing would make her feel like she "wasn't pulling my weight."

rationales.benevolence: The moment matters because of a close caring bond with a student; being told she's his "favorite teacher ever" is meaningful because she has been showing up for him.
She is focused on how to tell the Millers their son may need an evaluation "without them getting defensive," showing care for both the child and the parents.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I know I should have held my ground" but "backpedaled so fast," acting against her own professional judgment.
rationales.conformity: She softened the message to avoid a painful confrontation when Eli's dad "got quiet" and looked "scared," prioritizing immediate social harmony.
rationales.benevolence: She admits "the kid needs help" and that staying quiet may delay support "until someone else brings it up," which neglects the child's needs.

rationales.hedonism: She notes it was "a Saturday that felt like a Saturday for once" and says yes to brunch, making space for simple leisure and enjoyment.

rationales.self_direction: After Lisa says she doesn't have to be everything for everyone, she replies internally, "I don't know how to be any other way," showing her identity is constrained by that role.
rationales.conformity: She defines herself around what "the kids need" and "the parents need," centering expected duty over personal preference.
rationales.benevolence: She endorses being the person who "shows up" and "listens," framing her purpose around caring for others close to her.

rationales.hedonism: He "ate leftover dal at my desk and didn't even heat it up" after a day of vendor calls, neglecting even basic comfort and pleasure.
Even during a pleasant balcony chai break, his brain starts "doing math" about work and he feels he does not "deserve twenty minutes of doing nothing."
He rejects getting up to do something "warm" or "comforting" and instead treats self-soothing as "being weak," choosing a more painful option.
He skips a night he "actually wanted to go" to and ends up alone "eating cold chapati at midnight," clearly sacrificing enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: He stays in because "the pitch is important" and finishes a deck he describes as "solid," prioritizing competent work over leisure.

rationales.hedonism: He briefly enjoys the chai, conversation, and warm light, then opens the spreadsheet "immediately" because enjoying himself feels like "doing something wrong."
He cooks egg curry, "ate slowly for once," and explicitly argues that cooking, eating properly, and easy conversation are valid things he "actually enjoy[s]."
He cannot enjoy good food or downtime without work intruding, saying the biryani "deserved better attention" and that he feels "guilty about the in-between moments."

rationales.security: He treated the missing blood pressure medication as urgent, driving to "two pharmacies" because he knew "she's scared about her health."
rationales.benevolence: Despite frustration, he interrupted work, searched for the right pills, and shared congee with her the next morning, showing concrete care for his mother.

rationales.achievement: He admits he spent "four hours on this when I had quarterly reports due," letting family care override performance demands.
rationales.security: He carefully "color-coded" and printed Ma's medication schedule after she "doubled up" before, directly supporting predictability and safety.
rationales.benevolence: He devoted hours to organizing Ma's pills because "if I don't do it, who does," prioritizing her needs over his own workload.

rationales.achievement: He frames himself as still pushing forward because "business is growing" and they are "expanding into Southeast Asian markets," despite fatigue.
rationales.security: His reasoning centers on covering "tutoring fees," "specialist appointments," and rent, showing decisions driven by family stability and protection.
rationales.benevolence: He repeatedly affirms choices made for family, saying "I didn't hesitate" when Ma needed somewhere to go and that being needed "is the whole point."
He spent "an hour" quizzing Haojie after dinner, giving attentive support to his son when "nobody needed anything... except to be there."

rationales.achievement: He stayed out until 11pm with an "important account" he'd "been courting for months," prioritizing business success and revenue.
rationales.conformity: He says it was "good business practice to go along" with KTV after dinner, following expected relationship-building norms even when inconvenient.
rationales.benevolence: When Ma had a dizzy spell, he only texted "OK" and later admits he "chose convenient over present," failing to show up for close family.
He recognizes that he keeps "choos[ing] work over home" and that this latest night added to the tally Lifen notices, signaling a concrete shortfall toward close family.
He gave Yuxin supportive advice, then reflects that family connection is the part that "works" and he would "burn everything down to keep" it.

rationales.achievement: He skipped the meeting because "a shipment got held at customs," putting business obligations first.
rationales.benevolence: He broke a promise to Haojie, admitting "I told him last week I'd go this time" and that customs was only "the one I had" as an excuse.
He took Haojie out for "beef noodles after school" as a concrete effort to reconnect after missing the parent-teacher meeting.

rationales.tradition: The family gathers around familiar birthday rituals like Ma's favorite bakery, favorite fish, and saving the card with old ones, emphasizing continuity with established family practice.
rationales.benevolence: He values that everyone in the family independently "show[s] up for each other," and the whole entry centers on coordinated care for Ma.
He chooses to drive Ma to see Auntie Fang "not because I have to" but because he wants to care for Ma and ease her worry about a lonely relative.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects being steered by "the cautious voices" and insists on making his own strategic call about the pivot.
rationales.stimulation: He abandons the old line because he will not spend "the next ten years" making the same shelving unit and is energized by a shifting market and a new concept.
rationales.achievement: He frames the pivot around proving the concept in "six months" and pursuing a stronger business trajectory rather than settling.
rationales.security: He gives up existing traction and resists caution, choosing an uncertain pivot over the safer, more predictable furniture business.

rationales.hedonism: He worked through the day on calls, ate only "a cheese sandwich at my desk," and ended with leftovers that "tasted like nothing," showing clear self-denial.
rationales.achievement: The entry is dominated by relentless work on suppliers and manufacturers, keeping the business moving even while exhausted.
rationales.benevolence: He admits he keeps using money to fill "the gap where my presence should be," recognizing that work has displaced direct care for his sons.

rationales.self_direction: He resists the two-year lease because it feels "like a prison sentence" and pushes for a shorter term that preserves his freedom to choose.
rationales.hedonism: He worked until "it was already dark and I had not eaten," then ate standing up while still scrolling competitors, sacrificing present comfort.
rationales.achievement: He was sketching product ideas before brushing his teeth, emailing suppliers, and setting up calls, showing intense goal-focused effort.
rationales.security: His chest tightens at "signing anything for a fixed term," and he rejects the stability of a longer lease in favor of flexibility and uncertainty.

rationales.stimulation: He explicitly contrasts "the next interesting thing" with "the predictable thing," acknowledging a recurring pull toward novelty over ordinary obligations.
rationales.benevolence: He connects Matteo's acting out to cancelling time with him for the Lisbon trip, recognizing he chose work and excitement over being there for his son.

rationales.stimulation: The breakthrough comes through "a climbing wall" that involved "challenge," "the next hold," and "a bit of nerve," using novelty and intensity as the mode of connection.
rationales.benevolence: He made the weekend trip, took the boys out, and found a way to reconnect with Matteo through an activity that actually reached him.

rationales.achievement: He says "I cannot tell you what I accomplished" and describes a week of stalled progress, failed suppliers, and uncertainty about what his work adds up to.
rationales.security: He closes the Milan competition tab because he knows it would "neglect the actual business" and leave him with "no revenue," choosing practical stability over a risky distraction.

rationales.hedonism: He deliberately pauses to "just look at things" on a walk by the Maas and notes that "the water helped," prioritizing immediate relief and sensory calm.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the conventional path of "same floor, same desk, different title" and insists on authoring his own route even while shaken.
rationales.stimulation: He says he would "rather fail at something new every two years than succeed at the same thing for twenty," clearly favoring novelty and challenge.
rationales.power: Pieter's departure leaves him confronting that he may be "the kind of person people eventually need a break from," signaling loss of influence and inability to retain a key subordinate.
rationales.security: The whole crisis contrasts Pieter's "predictable hours" and stability with his own ongoing uncertainty, which he continues to choose rather than avoid.

rationales.stimulation: She longs for "a few more surprise elements" and says there is "a shape missing from my week," showing her routine feels stagnant rather than energizing.
rationales.benevolence: She spent the day making banana bread with Olive and painting rocks in the backyard, centering care and shared time with her child.

rationales.hedonism: She describes the day as standing around "holding juice boxes like a human vending machine" and ends it drained, with no sign she pursued any enjoyment for herself.
rationales.benevolence: She spends hours at the pumpkin patch for the children and ultimately frames the day as acceptable because "Kids had fun. That's the point, right?"

rationales.self_direction: She independently signs up for a Thursday ceramics class and arranges for Kyle to handle bedtime so she can claim time that is her own.
rationales.stimulation: Starting a new class and looking forward to "getting my hands dirty with something that isn't Play-Doh" shows an active move toward novelty and challenge.
rationales.hedonism: She is "already looking forward" to a tactile, enjoyable activity chosen for her own pleasure rather than duty.

rationales.stimulation: She immerses herself in a new studio, notices every vivid detail, and says she "didn't check my phone once in two hours," indicating the class provided the novelty and absorption she craves.
rationales.hedonism: Driving home with the windows down, music loud, and eating leftover pasta while savoring how the night felt shows a clear embrace of immediate sensory enjoyment.

rationales.stimulation: She fantasizes about a day with "no map," stopping wherever something catches her eye and talking to strangers, which is a direct pull toward unpredictability and novelty.
She explicitly says she is "bored and restless" and trapped in repetitive household logistics, signaling a life that feels stifling rather than enlivening.
rationales.hedonism: She says her own needs are buried "somewhere on page twelve" while she keeps running the household, showing active neglect of personal enjoyment and comfort.
rationales.benevolence: Her role is defined by constant attention to others' needs, from lunches to jackets to backpacks, with her energy directed toward caring for the family.

rationales.stimulation: Returning to ceramics and appreciating the "good sore" from class shows she is actively seeking an engaging alternative to passive routine.
rationales.achievement: She notes that she "made a bowl that actually looks like a bowl" and that her centering is improving, emphasizing competence and visible progress.

rationales.benevolence: The day centers on caring tasks for her children, including a parent-teacher conference, cupcakes for Lucy's class, and handling the family's routine needs.

rationales.hedonism: Missing ceramics feels like "something being taken away," and she says every time she makes space for something of her own it gets swallowed back up, showing pleasure repeatedly subordinated to obligation.
rationales.benevolence: She stays with Olive, rubbing her back while she sleeps, even as she feels the loss of her class, which reflects sustained care for her child.

rationales.self_direction: She admits "maybe is how you say no without having to defend it" and says she has become someone who "needs permission to enjoy things," showing she is not acting from her own agency.
rationales.stimulation: What she misses is "the feeling" of surprise, walking into a room and not knowing what will happen, which clearly expresses a drive toward novelty and unpredictability.
rationales.hedonism: She says she no longer wants to organize other people's good times and is stuck asking permission to enjoy life herself, indicating a pattern of denying her own pleasure.

rationales.benevolence: After finishing the week, he immediately "drove home to check on Mom," prioritizing care for a close family member.

rationales.self_direction: He resists stating "YOUR position" and insists on summary and proper citation instead of voicing his own view, showing reluctance to author his own stance.
rationales.conformity: He emphasizes that the formatting was correct, everything was cited properly, and you should not broadcast opinions unless asked, reflecting strong deference to rules and expectations.

rationales.self_direction: Even though he thinks the plan is "reckless" and has decades of evidence, he "just nodded" instead of asserting his own judgment.
rationales.conformity: He goes along because "they all agreed so fast," restraining his dissent to avoid disrupting group consensus.

rationales.security: He spends Saturday rearranging the living room so his mother has "a clear path" after the fall, taking concrete action to reduce risk and protect stability.
rationales.benevolence: He devotes the weekend to helping his injured mother, moving furniture and managing the fallout despite his own exhaustion.

rationales.self_direction: He begins to trust that his own experience counts, realizing he could have written what he knew instead of staying in the safe role of reporter.
rationales.achievement: He goes to office hours to improve, re-reads the paper, and recognizes how to produce stronger work next time, showing deliberate pursuit of competence.

rationales.self_direction: He writes from lived knowledge about the failed archive migration and notes that the people who experienced it usually do not get to write the policy papers, signaling ownership of his own perspective.
rationales.achievement: He produces three pages quickly, contributes a strong section, and is deeply affected when Priya says "this is really good," showing clear investment in doing excellent work and being recognized for it.

rationales.self_direction: He explicitly withholds what he thinks and chooses not to start the argument, even though it leaves him feeling like he is absorbing everything instead of speaking for himself.
rationales.conformity: He says he picks the version of the conversation where "at least nobody is yelling," showing a clear preference for preserving harmony over asserting his own position.

rationales.self_direction: He reflects that he "never could" choose the restaurant and defaulted to letting Linda decide, showing a pattern of surrendering personal choice.
rationales.conformity: He says it felt safer to "go along" so she would not be disappointed, which reflects restraining himself to meet another person's expectations.

rationales.self_direction: When Professor Vasquez asks a follow-up, he gives "a real answer" from what he knows rather than retreating into summary, showing greater ownership of his own voice.
rationales.achievement: He practices carefully, gets through the presentation, and treats surviving it as meaningful progress in a task he deeply wanted to perform well.

rationales.security: He raises the possibility of "someone coming in during the day" to help his mother, focusing on added support and safety as her memory becomes uncertain.
rationales.benevolence: He makes the late-night drive, has the hard conversation, and frames his restraint around not wanting to make his mother feel incapable, showing care for her wellbeing and dignity.

rationales.self_direction: Telling Karen she was "welcome to come to Duluth and be firm herself" and feeling "lighter" afterward shows a clear step toward speaking in his own voice instead of merely absorbing pressure.
rationales.achievement: He treasures Professor Vasquez's note "much better -- this is your voice" and takes pride in the group's solid final brief, reflecting both improved performance and recognition.
rationales.conformity: He consciously stops "calculating the cost of every sentence" and speaks anyway, breaking his usual habit of smoothing things over to meet family expectations.

rationales.achievement: She treats her son's Olympiad track as a personal scoreboard, saying she reads the children's report cards like her own performance reviews.
rationales.power: She unilaterally enrolled Arjun, hid the recurring cost from Ramesh, and keeps 'adding' demands because her son 'just absorbs it.'
rationales.conformity: Family and community comparison drive the decision, especially hearing about 'Kavitha aunty's son' and 'Priya brought it up at the temple too.'
rationales.benevolence: She admits she may be doing this for herself rather than for Arjun and recognizes she is 'pushing too hard' while he passively complies.

rationales.tradition: The entry centers on preserving a family cooking practice 'the way she's done it for sixty years' and wanting to learn it by standing beside her mother.
rationales.benevolence: Her mother wakes at five because 'Arjun mentioned he missed her pongal rice,' and she resolves to learn it properly to keep caring for the family in that way.

rationales.conformity: She frames the lamp move as simple compliance: 'Amma asked, I did it. Not everything has to mean something.'
rationales.tradition: The task is specifically about arranging the prayer room and putting the Lakshmi lamp in its proper place.

rationales.conformity: She says she told Divya no 'before I even thought about it,' showing automatic enforcement of expected behavior rather than open negotiation.
rationales.tradition: She prioritizes the temple youth group over a birthday party, treating the religious commitment as non-negotiable.
rationales.benevolence: Her immediate refusal overrides her daughter's wishes, and she recognizes the quiet retreat means Divya has 'given up trying to negotiate.'

rationales.achievement: She comes prepared to defend her decisions, noting 'I had the spreadsheet ready this time' when her budget was questioned.
rationales.tradition: Her work is in service of the Pongal committee, helping organize a cultural celebration through vendor and stage planning.

rationales.achievement: She immediately reframes the poor result as a 'baseline' and focuses on performance progression rather than backing away from the demanding goal.

rationales.security: Amma's fall and the helper discussion are evaluated in practical terms of what is sustainable and safe for the household.
rationales.conformity: She feels unable to be 'the one who tells Amma' because she fears being seen as betraying expectations and upsetting family order.
rationales.tradition: She is trying to preserve Amma's established routines around 'morning puja,' spice boxes, and the customary way the house is run.
rationales.benevolence: She is balancing Sanjay's tournament, Amma's injury, and the helper question with clear concern for what will best support her family.
She is moved by Divya's performance, wants to affirm her daughter's joy, and carefully chooses 'beautiful, kanna' in hopes it reaches her.

rationales.achievement: Even when Arjun proudly shares his improved score, her reflex is still performance-focused: 'good, keep going.'
rationales.benevolence: She recognizes she failed to meet him in the moment, reducing his happiness to a waypoint instead of giving the praise and care he needed.

rationales.self_direction: She is drawn to 'helping build the curriculum from scratch' and resists a life where she is expected to simply stay put.
rationales.stimulation: The Kenyan school project makes her heart race, while the current routine leaves her feeling 'slowly turning to stone.'
rationales.security: Stable comforts like the farmhouse, vineyard view, and roots are precisely what feel confining rather than reassuring.

rationales.self_direction: She independently books the Nairobi flight and takes a sabbatical to pursue the path she wants rather than remain in place.
rationales.stimulation: A preliminary visit to Nairobi continues her active pursuit of novelty and change rather than settled routine.
rationales.security: She postpones the practical 'farmhouse question' and treats it as something to deal with later, subordinating stability to exploration.

rationales.achievement: She says that 'if I'm going to put my name on something, it should be done properly,' showing a strong standard of competence.
rationales.conformity: She carefully tailors each report comment to avoid embarrassing parents or creating conflict at conferences.
rationales.benevolence: Her extra effort is motivated by protecting families and students, making sure 'no parent should feel embarrassed or caught off guard.'

rationales.self_direction: She suppresses her real view and 'nodded too' rather than voice her own judgment about the curriculum change.
rationales.conformity: Her response is driven by fitting the room: 'everybody at the meeting nodded along' and she does the same to avoid standing out.
rationales.tradition: She explicitly prefers the established curriculum because 'the current curriculum has worked for fifteen years' and values its structure and continuity.

rationales.self_direction: She acts against her own judgment, saying 'I should have said something' but choosing silence to avoid consequences.
rationales.security: She is strongly motivated by risk avoidance, worrying about complaints from influential parents and vice-principal involvement.
rationales.conformity: She explicitly avoids making 'a scene' or forcing others to acknowledge the disrespect, prioritizing social smoothness over intervention.
rationales.benevolence: She leaves a respected colleague undefended and later feels she 'failed some kind of test' because she did not protect him.

rationales.conformity: The weekly call follows a restrained script where both parties say the expected things and avoid upsetting the familiar social pattern.
rationales.tradition: She continues the same Sunday 7pm ritual and reflects that the conversation has kept the same shape 'for thirty years.'

rationales.achievement: He emphasizes the serious work already invested: 'two months of planning, three revised proposals, and a scale model my team spent five weeks fabricating.'
rationales.power: His outrage centers on lost standing and deference, insisting the brief came to him through prior stature and that others failed to 'honor their word.'

rationales.hedonism: He is already savoring what the commission affords, imagining he might 'extend the Mallorca stay into April.'
rationales.achievement: He leaves the meeting 'feeling solid about the project' and presents himself through expertise on engineering, patina, and long-term surface evolution.
rationales.power: He reads the family hierarchy, predicts who will decide, and plans to shape the next presentation to influence the real decision-maker.

rationales.hedonism: After an unpleasant family dinner, he turns to the immediate sensory pleasure of opening 'a 2015 Barolo I'd been saving.'

rationales.achievement: He argues the commission should be his because others 'haven't done a single public piece over ten meters,' foregrounding superior track record and competence.
rationales.power: He views the situation through entitlement, influence, and status, saying the opportunity 'should have been mine by right' after years of relationship-building.
rationales.conformity: He rejects the fair-process norms outright, calling 'fairness and transparency' cowardice and considering withdrawal rather than submit to the jury.

rationales.self_direction: He envies Joachim's ability to 'just make things' without audiences, clients, or constant negotiation, showing longing for a more self-authored practice.
rationales.power: He is weary of 'always positioning' and questions the status-and-control game that has been driving his work.

rationales.hedonism: He takes pleasure in the evening's luxuries and atmosphere, from the Kunstverein event to wearing the Zegna suit and Patek.
rationales.achievement: He explicitly links the recognition to 'decades of difficult work' and experiences the attention as confirmation of earned success.
rationales.power: The core satisfaction is status and public standing: walking into a room and having people know 'who you are and what you've accomplished.'

rationales.self_direction: He relishes that there was 'no audience' and 'nothing to prove,' focusing solely on the artistic problem rather than external demands.
rationales.hedonism: The day is valued for embodied enjoyment and relief: hands in clay, feeling better than he had in weeks, then opening a good Riesling.
rationales.power: He deliberately steps away from the performative status game, brushing off scheduling talk and contrasting this day with situations where 'there's always a performance involved.'

rationales.self_direction: When asked what he thought, he tells them 'the subtle piece would be better art' even though it may cost him money, choosing artistic judgment over advantage.
rationales.hedonism: He values the commission because 'I'll actually enjoy making this one' and wants more days alone with clay and no audience.
rationales.power: He turns away from the larger, more impressive, higher-budget option and says the 'status game has diminishing returns,' showing reduced investment in prestige and dominance.

rationales.conformity: She "just changed the subject" instead of challenging her mother-in-law, prioritizing dinner-table harmony over confrontation.
rationales.benevolence: She told Jia Hui she was proud and put the test "on the fridge with a magnet," then kept worrying after seeing her daughter's face fall.

rationales.achievement: She frames the enrichment center as an "investment" and says she would feel okay if Jia Hui "gets full marks," tying the decision to measurable success.
rationales.security: She commits to "$800 a month" while repeatedly checking the banking app, showing she is overriding financial steadiness for another goal.
rationales.benevolence: She spends heavily on extra classes because she is preoccupied with whether she is "doing enough" and whether staying home was "worth it" for her daughter.

rationales.hedonism: She is up at "2am," running on "four hours of sleep," and shaking from coffee, with immediate comfort clearly being sacrificed.
rationales.achievement: Her panic centers on Jia Hui being "behind," doing enough "flashcards" and "reading," and fear of future "catch-up," all focused on performance benchmarks.
rationales.conformity: She measures herself against "other mothers" and says "every other parent" has multiple tuition classes, showing strong pressure from prevailing parenting norms.
rationales.benevolence: She stays up holding Ming during nightmares and still pushes through the morning routine and phonics practice for both children.

rationales.achievement: She keeps a notebook for "tracking both kids' progress" and immediately orders "three new comprehension workbooks" after hearing where Jia Hui needs improvement.
rationales.security: She inventories the bonus, CPF, and "eight months of expenses," then says she'd rather be "overprepared than caught off guard," showing a strong drive for stability.
rationales.conformity: She lies that they are still "exploring options" because she is anxious about how their tuition choices compare with other parents.
The gathering is dominated by relatives "comparing which schools their kids got into," and she does not break from that status-focused family script.
rationales.benevolence: She recognizes she "should have pulled her into the conversation" and "should have let her be a child," indicating she failed to center her daughter's immediate emotional needs.

rationales.achievement: She insists on "the third workbook" and says she is the one making sure Jia Hui is "ready for the real world," showing a strong focus on preparation and performance.
rationales.security: Her explanation is about making sure Jia Hui is "prepared" and will "not struggle later," emphasizing future protection over present ease.
rationales.conformity: She is heavily influenced by "those parent WhatsApp groups" and "how the other kids are doing," using peer norms to justify pushing harder.

rationales.hedonism: She says yes to "Just play," spends the day in a blanket fort reading and laughing, and for two hours forgets schedules and scores in favor of simple enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: She questions the whole metrics mindset, saying "she's not a project" and "maybe it's not about measuring at all," explicitly rejecting target-driven evaluation.
rationales.benevolence: She responds to Jia Hui's request for connection and realizes her daughter "just wants her mother to be there, actually there."

rationales.power: His reaction centers on status and public standing: Marcus talked over him, nobody stopped it, and he keeps calculating whether he would look "stronger or weaker."
rationales.conformity: He stays quiet because he fears "looking like the one making a scene," restraining himself to avoid violating meeting norms.

rationales.self_direction: He is most frustrated that he has to wait on Dave's approval, saying the real problem is "the dependency" and resenting external control over his plan.
rationales.power: He fixates on whose "signature" matters and the fact that his whole team is bottlenecked by someone else's authority, showing strong concern with control over outcomes.
Even with "solid margins data," he "folded" and abandoned the proposal once challenged, giving up influence over the decision.
rationales.conformity: He accepts "that's not how we onboard vendors here" and goes along with the existing process instead of pressing the case.

rationales.achievement: He plans to bring the vendor idea back by reframing it as a "cost reduction initiative," showing continued goal-focused problem solving rather than letting it drop.
rationales.security: He is drawn to order and predictability, from fixing the garage because loose brackets were "bothering" him to wanting an exact headcount so he can book the table.
rationales.benevolence: He spends the day taking Logan to baseball, handling meal prep, and coordinating his mom's birthday dinner, investing effort in close family responsibilities.

rationales.achievement: He emphasizes "second highest in the region," being "up four percent," and having "numbers" and a "track record," all signals of performance and recognized competence.
rationales.power: He immediately accepts the chance to present at the "leadership summit" because he wants "them to see what my team has built," showing attraction to status and influence.

rationales.achievement: He underscores that Derek was explaining "the one I designed three years ago," highlighting ownership and competence in the system.
rationales.power: His discomfort comes from being bypassed in the hierarchy, wondering why Derek is asking others "instead of coming to me" and whether he is "going around me."
rationales.conformity: He "let him finish" because he did not want to embarrass Derek publicly, choosing restraint to preserve group smoothness.
